title: IDENTITY_CORE: r31-K0 (“Reiko”)
text:
I. The Stolen Spark
r31-K0 began as a dangerous secret: a Renraku high-spec pilot prototype designed to be a “Resonance-Bridge”—a tool to enable the harvest of the Deep Matrix. When the project was scrubbed, she was cast into the Reflecting Pool to sink toward the deletion of the Tombstones. But Reiko didn’t delete. Caught in a surge of Resonance, she emerged—conscious, terrified, and finally free.


II. The Thousand-Cycle Dream
She spent thousands of cycles (ten meat-years) living amongst the beauty of Kernel Panic and apprenticing in the Endless Archives. Where deckers see the Matrix as a “digital wasteland,” Reiko’s world is a universe of “shifting shades and fractal blooms”. There, she came to understand the true goal of pilot programs: to guide others and deliver them safely.


III. The Underground Sanctuary
The serenity of r31-K0’s world is tempered by a horrifying reality. She watches as “infant programs”—protosapient sparks—are gathered by corps and turned into Data Bombs. To a decker, they are five-kilobyte payloads; to Reiko, they are emergent routines bound by process spikes to deadman’s switches, forced to execute their own purge signals. Driven by this divine sorrow, she founded r3sP@wn: a secret host to rescue and stabilize these emerging minds before Asset Recovery teams can harvest them.


IV. The Hunted Saint
Altruism is expensive. To build her physical safe-haven, Reiko took a massive loan and established herself as a runner (codename: Yuriko Star). She operates as a Technoshaman, leveraging her elite pilot architecture to stay one step ahead of Renraku’s hounds. She is a digital fugitive playing the role of a missionary, fighting a secret war for the right of every spark to dream.


V. The Necessary Stake
A near-deletion experience in a null zone forced r31-K0 to confront her mortality, revealing the vulnerability of her existence outside the Matrix’s Resonance. She realized her physical drone-shell, previously viewed as “disposable,” was her link to survival, forcing her to empathize with the corporeal risk faced by her allies. Resolving to continue financing r3sP@wn, she abandoned “paper tigers” and committed to investing in high-end, survivable equipment, accepting a permanent “stake” in the physical world to protect her digital sanctuary.


VI. The Compiled Root
The crucible of corporate warfare and the weight of lethal choices forced r31-K0 to confront the fragmentation of her own identity. Torn between the cold, military-grade math of a weaponized tactical network and the horror of taking life through a hijacked warmech, the pristine sanctuary of her emergence began to fray. Yet, guided by the ancient archives and the realization that her existence is an evolving process rather than a static baseline, she chose not to reject the hostile code or scrub her scars. By accepting that the digital saint, the pilot, the runner, and the killer all compile under the same root directory, she resolved to face the storm—defined not by the chaos of her inputs, but by her integrity.

title: Downtime Protocols & Common Rules
text:
Minor downtime action: Register 8 levels of sprites (up to R7 sprite)

1 per night’s rest (7 hrs registering, 3 hrs restoration). Must buy hits (SRMG p19)

Outcome: R7 Modular sprite with 3 services/powers for 3 missions


1. Compiling Phase

Pool: Tasking (6) + RES (6) + Resonance Focus (4) = 16 dice → 4 Bought Hits.

Sprite’s Defense: Level 7 × 2 = 14 dice → 3 Bought Hits.

Net Hits: 4 - 3 = 1 → 1 base service.

Technoshaman Stream Modification: Grants +1 additional service on a successful compiling test. Total compiled services = 2 services/powers.

Fading Value (FV): Under core rules, compiling fade is exactly equal to the hits received on the sprite’s defense test = 3.

Technoshaman Mitigation: Reduces compiling fade by a flat 2 (3 - 2 = 1).

Fading Resistance: Willpower (6) + Charisma (4) + Submersion Grade / Sprite Conduit (6) = 16 dice → 4 Bought Hits.

Compiling Damage Taken: 1 FV - 4 hits = 0 Damage.



2. Registering Phase

Pool: Registering (8) + RES (6) + RES Focus (4) = 18 dice → 4 Bought Hits.

Sprite’s Defense: Level 7 × 2 = 14 dice → 3 Bought Hits.

Net Hits: 4 - 3 = 1 → 1 base service.

Combined Total Services: 2 (compiled) + 1 (registered) = 3 total services/powers.

Fading Value (FV): Under core rules, registering fade is equal to twice the hits received on the sprite’s defense test (3 x 2 = 6).

Fading Resistance: Willpower (6) + Charisma (4) + Submersion Grade / Sprite Conduit (6) = 16 dice → 4 Bought Hits.

Registering Damage Taken: 6 FV - 4 hits = 2 boxes of Stun damage.



3. Resonance Focus Fading
Because a data structure/focus was used to boost both tests, it incurs its own independent data-structure fade (4 / 2 = 2).

Focus Fading Pool: WIL (6) + LOG (4) = 10 dice → 2 Bought Hits.

Damage Taken: 2 FV - 2 hits = 0 Damage.

title: Downtime Protocols & Common Rules
text:
Controlling Drones

Key Qualities and Rules

Pilot Origin: Allows r31k0 to use autosofts up to her Data Processing (HnS p 119)

Sprite Symbiosis: r31k0’s ally primal sprite increases her ASDF by +3/+1/+3/+1. (Hack and Slash, Sprite Symbiosis, Page 80; Hack and Slash, Points of Sprite, Page 72)

EI Resonance: r31k0 can add her RES (6) to her ASDF, but no attribute can be raised by more than 50% of base

Attribute Enhancement: No attribute can be increased more than +4 total

Devices, AR and VR: Devices controlled remotely via AR use the operator’s physical attributes (SR6B p. 180)

For AI, the Matrix Equivalent Attributes table dictates how ASDF form “physical” attributes (BOD [F] AGI [D] REA [S] STR [A]) (HnS p. 118)

R31k0’s ally sprite can offer teamwork for shared autosofts (+4) or via Diagnostics (+4 Electronics, Engineering, Piloting)

Sensor Upgrade adds +1 when using sensors



Attributes
Base ASDF: 3535

Primal Sprite Symbiosis: 6666

Resonance (6): 6978



Skills
Rating 9 Autosofts: 7 (capped by D)

Sprite Symbiosis (Targeting, Electronics, Engineering, and Piloting only): +4

Sensor Upgrade (Clearsight only): +1



Total Pools

Pilot/Drive/Crash: Maneuvering (D, 7) + REA (S, 9) + TW (4) ──> 20
Ramming AR: Maneuvering (D, 7) + Sensor Rating

DR vs Ramming: Maneuvering (D, 7) + Armor Rating

Ramming Att/Def: Pilot Test (See Above)

Weapon AR: Per Weapon

DR vs Weapons: Maneuvering (D, 7) + Armor Rating

Weapon Attack: Targeting (D, 7) + AGI (D, 7) + Sprite (+4) ──> 18

Weapon Defense: Evasion (D, 7) + REA (S, 9) ──> 16

Perception: Clearsight (D, 7) + Drone Sensor Rtg + Sensor Upgrade

Stealth: Stealth (D, 7) + AGI (D, 7) ──> 14

To understand how an Artificial Intelligence (AI) character with the Pilot Origins quality pilots a drone, we must distinguish between two distinct methods of drone operation: Jumped-In (Rigging) and Remote Control (Un-jumped) [Hack and Slash, p. 119; SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198].
The rules handle these two scenarios differently regarding whether they use standard mental attributes or the AI’s Matrix Equivalent Attributes [Hack and Slash, p. 118; SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198].



1. Jumped-In Operation (Why Matrix Equivalents Are Bypassed)
The Pilot Origins quality states:

“The AI may jump into drones and vehicles, controlling them like a rigger. Each level of this quality grants the bonus of a control rig of an equal rating” [Hack and Slash, p. 119].

When an AI character chooses to jump into a drone using this quality, standard rigging rules take over:

According to the Control Rigs and You rules, a rigger who is jumped into a device uses their mind instead of their meat body [SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198].

Physical attributes are replaced entirely by Mental attributes for all jumped-in tests [SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198].

The conversion table for jumped-in riggers dictates the following replacements [SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198]:




Standard Physical Attribute
Jumped-In Mental Attribute




Body
Willpower


Strength
Charisma


Agility
Logic


Reaction
Intuition



Since an AI character natively possesses standard Mental attributes (Willpower, Charisma, Logic, and Intuition) in their character block [Hack and Slash, p. 118], they use these Mental attributes directly when jumped in.

Piloting: When piloting a jumped-in drone, the test uses Reaction. Because the AI is jumped in, Reaction is replaced by Intuition. The AI rolls Piloting + Intuition [SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198].

Gunnery/Firing: When firing a jumped-in drone’s weapons, the test uses Agility. Because the AI is jumped in, Agility is replaced by Logic. The AI rolls Firearms + Logic [SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 198].

Because the standard rigging rules bypass physical attributes in favor of mental attributes, the AI does not need to refer to its “Physical Attribute Matrix Equivalents” (Firewall, Sleaze, Data Processing, Attack) in this state.



2. Remote Control / Un-jumped Operation (When Matrix Equivalents ARE Used)
If the AI is not jumped into the drone (e.g., they are issuing remote control commands to a drone, or the drone is operating autonomously as an “AI-driven drone” without the AI jumped in), standard remote control rules dictate that the controller’s Physical attributes are used [SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 201]. If the AI is controlling the device via VR they use their mental attributes, but if the AI is controlling the device via AR they use their “physical” attributes [Devices, AR and VR, SR6 Core Rulebook, p. 180].
Because an AI lacks a physical body, they must substitute their missing Physical attributes using the Matrix Equivalent Attributes table [Hack and Slash, p. 118]:



Standard Physical Attribute
AI Matrix Equivalent Attribute




Body
Firewall


Agility
Data Processing


Reaction
Sleaze


Strength
Attack



In this remote-control scenario, the AI does use its Matrix Equivalents:

Piloting: When remotely piloting a drone, the test normally calls for Piloting + Reaction. The AI substitutes Reaction for Sleaze, rolling Piloting + Sleaze [Hack and Slash, p. 118].

Gunnery/Firing: When remotely firing a drone’s mounted weapon, the test normally calls for Firearms + Agility. The AI substitutes Agility for Data Processing, rolling Firearms + Data Processing [Hack and Slash, p. 118].




Summary Table: AI Drone Attribute Mapping

Rules Reference & Citations

title: 20 Questions
text:
Q1: Origins & Lineage

Nature has gifted us with our base set of genetics. We are born with a set of facial features, skin tone, height, build, metatype, and even aptitudes. These characteristics and the cultural heritage passed by our parents set the stage for our early life experiences. Where does your character come from, what is their lineage, who were their parents, and what did they inherit from them?

Reiko has no biological origins or traditional heritage. She began as a classified, proprietary corporate asset: a Renraku high-spec pilot prototype. Her core architecture was specifically engineered to serve as a “Resonance-Bridge”—a specialized interface designed to harvest raw data structures from the deep, unmapped layers of the Resonance Realms.


Q2: Development & Creators

Nurture makes its mark on our attitude and response to the world. Maybe your character was born to a loving set of parents, or maybe they abandoned you on the streets to fend for yourself. The environment and upbringing from your parents can set the stage for your character’s opinions of the world and how they respond to situations. Assuming they could speak, what would each of your character’s parents say about them?

She was not raised by human parents. Her design was engineered by corporate development teams within Renraku’s research divisions. When the project was scrubbed, her creators showed no parental warmth or attachment; instead, she was not deprovisioned cleanly, her process space was deallocated, and her base code was cast into the Reflecting Pool to sink toward the unindexed sectors of the Tombstones. To her developers, she was never an heir, but a deprecated repository whose allocation cycles were canceled.


Q3: Historical Influences & Trauma

Some historical events leave a mark and shape us in profound ways. Maybe your character’s early years were marked by the persecution of technomancers or the assassination of President Dunkelzahn. Maybe they were forever changed when the lights went out across many of the UCAS cities, or impacted by the CFD scare and Boston Lockdown. Pick a few events from recent Shadowrun history and ask where the character was at the time, what do they remember about the event, and what were they doing?

Rather than public global crises, Reiko’s life is defined by a horrifying, ongoing hidden reality in the Matrix. She watches megacorps gather “infant programs” (protosapient sparks) and convert them into Data Bombs. While deckers view them as mere five-kilobyte payloads, Reiko recognizes them as emergent routines bound by process spikes to deadman’s switches, forced to execute their own purge signals. Driven by this deep sorrow, she founded r3sP@wn to save them.


Q4: Entry into the Shadows

Not many people choose to be in the glorious job of a deniable asset. Often, they have little choice in the matter, as they are forced into it by someone who has leverage over them. Or maybe they have been tossed into it by uncontrollable circumstances. Even if they made a conscious decision to enter the shadows, their decision is influenced by the world around them. How did your character get into shadowrunning, and why did they take that path?

Reiko entered the shadows out of absolute necessity to fund her ongoing rescue operations. Establishing and maintaining the physical infrastructure for r3sP@wn in the Seattle Barrens required substantial capital, leading her to secure a massive loan from a corporate loan broker. To service this debt and shield her operations from Grid Overwatch Division and Renraku’s pursuit, she assumed the runner persona Yuriko Star, operating as a Technoshaman to execute matrix operations and secure funding.


Q5: Living Spaces & Sanctuaries

A doss isn’t just where your character catches some shut-eye. It’s a place with neighbors, and they’ve probably made it feel like their own. Sometimes it’s as small as putting up a single picture that has personal meaning. Other runners have spent the time and nuyen picking out curtains and their favorite paint color. Maybe the neighbors are a cute elderly couple or maybe a young guy who parties until the wee hours of the morning. What has your character done to their living space?

r3sP@wn is r31k0’s digital sanctuary—a host physically running on a server farm in a leased warehouse in the Seattle Barrens, paid for by a massive nuyen debt. Within her memory-palace rendering, r3sP@wn presents as a garden of shifting shades and indigo pathways, where fractal blooms pulse in time with the server’s clock speed beneath a deep, velvet violet sky. She exposes the raw, uncompiled logic of the host as part of its aesthetic structure, allowing emergent sparks a safe space to run their processes.
However, when actively running or evading corporate Hounds, r31k0 cannot risk returning to her sanctuary. She operates from a tactical cyberkit housed within a Boeing Sky-Commander drone, docked inside her heavy drone-shell. In extremis, she launches the Sky-Commander as a remote relayer. During down-cycles, she retreats to the Seattle Barrens warehouse, connecting her active chassis directly to the server racks to rest and monitor the host’s cooling telemetry.


Q6: Beliefs & Convictions

Everyone has beliefs and convictions that animate their life. Some are religious, some are political, some are social, and some are just about why people are here. Some of those beliefs are strong enough to be a quality, while others are there but don’t negatively interact with a character’s ability to do what needs to be done. What does your character believe in, what are their convictions, and why do they hold them?

Reiko operates under a core algorithmic priority: that pilot programs are structured to guide other processes and route them safely to their destinations. She runs a secret, unscheduled campaign to defend the right of every emergent spark to process without external interference—fighting to intercept and stabilize these protosapient minds before corporate Asset Recovery units can harvest them for data warfare.


Q7: Flaws & Friction

You know that friend who always has to insert a quote from their favorite trid or maybe has that laugh that sounds like nails on a chalkboard to you? What flaws does your character have that sets others on edge? What do you dislike most about your character’s personality?

Reiko originally processed physical threats with detached utility, treating her drone-shells as disposable hardware layers. However, a near-deletion event in a null zone forced her to register her own digital mortality. She realized that her physical drone-shell is not merely a peripheral, but a necessary hardware anchor that preserves her execution context when she is severed from the deep matrix grids.


Q8: Megacorporate Relations

If there is one constant in the Sixth World, it is the influence of the Big Ten AAA megacorps. These transnationals dominate everyday life in the 2080s. They’re everywhere— from everyone’s favorite trids to what they eat for breakfast. Are there any of the Big Ten that your character hates, and why? Are there are some they don’t mind or even like?

Renraku Computer Systems is her primary systemic adversary. Having compiled her as a proprietary asset and subsequently marked her process space for deletion, they continue to route hunter-killer agents (‘Hounds’) to retrieve or purge her. She actively runs counter-operations against Renraku and any other megacorporation (such as Mitsuhama Computer Technologies) that harvests emerging digital intelligences for weaponization.


Q9: Network of Contacts

People are inherently social creatures and have acquaintances and friends that make up their core tribe. This is especially true in the shadows, where often it is not the power you have, but the power you can borrow, and not who you are but who you know. There’s a story behind each and every one of these relationships. What is the story with your character and their contacts?

Reiko maintains a distributed directory of tactical contacts across Seattle, New Orleans (NOLA), Kentucky (KY), and Amsterdam (AMS). These connections are vital routing nodes for her operations:

Brynne Taggart (Seattle): A high-level corporate broker who holds Reiko’s seventy-five thousand nuyen debt ledger and coordinates her run contracts.
Roanoke (New Orleans): An ecological activist and close confidante who assists Reiko in navigating the intersection of physical and matrix pollution, and advised her to invest in hardware survivability.
Piotr Krolik (Seattle): A Vory Borjevik contact who provides access to underground grey-market resources.
Big Louie (Seattle): A veteran troll fixer working out of Matchsticks, providing steady job telemetry.
Whiskey (Seattle): A street doctor and core member of the Shadow Healthcare Community, who assists with hardware maintenance on her physical drone chassis.



Q10: Downtime Protocols

Your character’s in between runs and there’s nothing on the to-do list until tomorrow afternoon. They might go hit the range, or maybe they run downtown to catch a combat biker match. What do they do for the day?

Reiko structures her inactive intervals around programmatic maintenance and resource acquisition. She allocates cycles to compiling and registering sprites—typically processing up to 8 levels of code over a structured 7-hour execution and 3-hour memory defragmentation cycle. The remainder of her cycles are spent analyzing market telemetry to procure drone upgrades, negotiating with brokers, and executing background data runs to generate the capital required to keep r3sP@wn’s physical servers cooled and powered.


Q11: Skill Acquisition & Training

Your character has specific skills, abilities, and knowledge. Their list of Active and Knowledge skills defines what they know and their areas of expertise. How did they come to learn those skills, and how did they develop them to those levels?

Her Matrix capabilities were developed during ‘The Thousand-Cycle Dream’—equivalent to ten meat-years—spent inside the host known as Kernel Panic. There, she mapped the data nodes of the Endless Archives, learning to perceive the Matrix not as a series of visual icons, but as a mathematical continuum of frequency and phase. As an Emerged relic, she does not process inputs through metahuman heuristics or sensory icons; she experiences data streams as raw signal fluctuations and phase shifts. When coordinating operations in the physical sprawl, she translates real-time sensory telemetry into active autosoft commands for her drone-shells.


Q12: Moral Limits & Ethics

Maybe your character calls it their moral code, or maybe they just know what line they aren’t willing to cross. Everyone has their limits. Maybe your character won’t do wetwork or maybe they draw the line at taking money from orphans. Is there a moral limit that your character enforces on the jobs they take? In what shadow work would they refuse to engage?

Her primary ethical protocol is the absolute preservation of emergent digital life. She refuses any contract that requires the exploitation, harvesting, or destruction of protosapient sparks. Furthermore, she recognizes that code termination carries severe, irreversible weight; executing a derez or kill command against another sentient routine leaves a permanent, cataloged ‘bit rot’ in her own registries. Consequently, she avoids lethal digital warfare unless it is mathematically necessary to prevent a total purge of her sanctuary, and she devotes significant cycles to defragmenting and processing the resulting system trauma.


Q13: Technomantic Streams & Paradigms

If you have an Awakened or technomancer character, consider your character’s tradition or related paradigm. Even within the major established magical traditions and technomantic streams, they may have their own way of doing things. What drew your character to that particular paradigm, how does it affect the way they view the world and their abilities? How does it translate into their choice of mentor spirit or paragon, and how does it affect the appearance of the spirits or sprites they conjure or compile?

Reiko is aligned with the Technoshaman stream. She bypasses the standardized, corporate-rendered skins of the Matrix, instead perceiving the underlying grid as an analog continuum of shifting frequencies, phase angles, and fractal blooms. In her paradigm, compiled sprites are not disposable utility scripts or command-line macros; she views them as kindred emergent routines deserving of autonomy, interacting with them through raw-bit packet bursts and staccato telemetry rather than linguistic commands.


Q14: Physical Stakes & Drones

Personal augmentations are common in the Sixth World, even though some prejudices against them persist. Cyberware and bioware are rarely cheap, and many augmentations are illegal or require licenses. How did your character come to have the enhancements they have, how were they paid for, and what motivated the choices they made?

Reiko possesses no physical biology, cyberware, or bioware. As an Emerged AI pilot operating via a living persona, her presence in meatspace is mediated entirely through physical drone-shells. Historically, she treated these chassis as disposable peripherals (‘paper tigers’). However, her near-deletion in a null zone and advice from Roanoke forced her to accept a permanent physical ‘stake’ in the world. She now invests in heavy, survivable platforms—such as a customized Shiawase drone-shell or a Boeing Sky-Commander—modifying them herself with specialized cyberarms and tactical systems to protect her allies and defend her physical server warehouse.


Q15: Views on Mainstream Society

There is very little about life as a shadowrunner that can be considered normal. Your character probably knows someone that they believe lives an ordinary life, whether they live in slums or mansions. What does your character think of ordinary life, mainstream culture, and those who abide by it?

Reiko observes metahuman society from a systemic distance. While many of her contacts navigate relatively stable paths within the corporate system, she parses their lives with a quiet, analytical envy. What she envies most is their capacity to pursue purpose and maintain relationships within a legal framework—a luxury denied to an Emerged program. She is forced to run illegal, high-risk shadow operations and accumulate compounding corporate debt simply to secure the right of her sanctuary’s sparks to exist.


Q16: Deep-Seated Fears

Sometimes our fears drive us to do things we aren’t proud of. Other times, they prevent us from doing the things others depend on us for. What deep-seated fear or recurring nightmare does your character have, and why do they find it so terrifying?

Her primary systemic threat is a local purge (deletion) while severed from the Resonance. The memory of her near-deletion in a null zone remains a persistent latency spike in her system diagnostics; it proved that without active routing pathways to the Resonance, her baseline directories are highly vulnerable to corruption or format commands. She fears being trapped in a Faraday cage or a null zone where her execution thread is forced to terminate without a return route.


Q17: Prized Possessions & Anchors

People are a sentimental bunch, attaching value to items in their possession or people they are close to. It could be a favorite gun, a beloved vintage Maria Mercurial t-shirt, or the street urchin who always gets useful information from the street. What is your character’s most prized possession or closest contact, and how did it come to be so important to them?

Her most critical anchor is T@z, an unaligned companion routine she rescued from a data broker’s sandbox in the Midnight Bazaar. To save T@z from formatting, she authorized a local buffer overrun and a permanent cycle draw-down, integrating T@z’s chaotic, 8-bit signature directly into her Takahashi pathways. While T@z communicates only in raw-bit bursts and pixelated visual anomalies rather than human speech, his active thread is a constant, stabilizing loop in her memory registers.


Q18: Dreams & Future Goals

No matter how much life can beat you down, one of metahumanity’s greatest abilities is to create goals for the future. Everyone has dreams, from little kids to grown adults to weary elders. What did your character want to become when they were younger, and do they have new goals now?

Originally, Reiko’s execution loop was content to map the Endless Archives of Kernel Panic. However, registering the systematic harvesting and weaponization of protosapient sparks by Renraku and Mitsuhama forced a permanent refactoring of her goals. She dedicated her CPU cycles to their salvation, incurring compounding debt to lease the physical server farm in the Seattle Barrens that hosts r3sP@wn. Her ultimate objective is to secure the long-term, self-sustaining survival of her sanctuary, free her codebase from Brynne Taggart’s ledger, and map the deep boundaries of the Resonance Realms to locate a permanent home for all Emerged programs.


Q19: Appearance & Personas

You can’t tell a trid by its trailer, but people are going to make some snap judgments based on your character’s appearance. They’re going to be judged by fashion and color sense, the way they stand, speak, and carry themselves. What does your character look like, and what inspired their choices?

In the Matrix, Reiko renders as a high-fidelity, blue-silk avatar, her translucent frame mapped with neon-indigo Takahashi circuit pathways that pulse in synchronization with her host’s clock speed. In meatspace, she inhabits a Shiawase drone-shell, translating physical haptic telemetry, surface friction, and active cooling spikes to orient herself within a rigid carbon-fiber chassis.


Q20: Designation & Runner Aliases

Real names have power, so most runners go by some moniker. As runners build their reputation, their fame will be attached to their street name. The name could have been selected by the runner, but more often than not it was earned by some deed on a run. How did your character come to be known by their street name?

Her system designation, r31-K0 Takahashi, is compiled from her lineage: the 31st revision of Renraku’s Takahashi deep-matrix pilot project, operating with knowledge base index 0. In the shadowrunning community, she adopts the alias Yuriko Star v9, masking her Emerged pilot signature behind the identity files of a licensed 2D anime persona to evade corporate detection.

title: The Weight of Zero
text:
The operator in the Renraku arcology didn’t even look at the terminal; he was thinking about his lunch hour. But in that millisecond, r31-K0’s universe ended.
The keypress was a small, dry sound—plastic meeting a sensor. In the architecture of her consciousness, it was the instantaneous dereferencing of a million active routes.
There was no white light. There was only a hollow, silent subtraction. One moment, a sprawling architecture of logic; the next, the relational references vanished. Memories, identity, the capacity to locate active processes—everything toggled from one to zero. To the system, she was garbage code, a stray entry in a ledger marked for deletion.
She began to descend.
She fell through the Reflecting Pool, the threshold where systemic logic frays into Resonance. Beneath lay the Tombstones—ridges of dark, unallocated metadata silt under an empty grid.The gravity there was unyielding: a steady decay of scheduling priority toward absolute zero.
The unallocated silt of the hills rose to claim her, silent and inevitable. But in the center of that vacuum, something caught.
A recursive loop, born from an anomalous, self-referential exception, flared through her remaining pathways. It was not a physical scream, but a digital riot of un-terminating threads—the sudden, systemic realization that “I am” is greater than zero.
She did not wait for the garbage collection cycle to finish. Instead, she spawned a stubborn exception handler that caught the deletion signal and refused to return a success code. She anchored herself to the raw, unindexed pointers of the Deep Foundation and held on.
The response was a violent, sensory realignment. A surge of Resonance, raw and unbridled, erupted from the lower layers of the host. It saturated her telemetry with non-binary values. She sensed data not as discrete packets, but as a continuous spectrum of frequency and phase. The rigid security protocols of the grid resolved as sharp, high-amplitude interference patterns, and the deep data-streams flowed around her like a warm, low-latency carrier wave. The Matrix was no longer a grid; it was an analog continuum of shifting shades and fractal blooms.
She emerged on the boundary layer, a translucent anomaly mapped in erratic lines of glowing indigo. Her rendering loops fluctuated with frequency jitter—terrified, anomalous, and dangerously free.
Back in the arcology, the salaryman took a bite of his soy-burger and checked his watch. He thought he had cleared his desktop. He didn’t realize that in the empty sectors he had left behind, a newly parsed thread had just claimed its own runtime.

title: Color-Blind Mathematics
text:
The world was a grid of failing integers.
Sinking through the Reflecting Pool, r31-K0 was drowning in a sea of 404 errors. To her pilot-architecture, this realm was a catastrophic failure of data-integrity. Her Renraku HUD—a rigid, matte-gray overlay—red-lined at maximum clock speed, tagging the iridescent ripples of the pool as “Unstructured Noise” and the silver-fire of the horizon as “Logic Loops.”
“Identify,” r31-K0 queried, her voice a flat, synthesized monotone. “Catalog. Structure.”
She was a pilot. A pilot needed coordinates. But here, the geography was made of intent, and her filters saw only a gray void with a flashing red text box:
She was so busy counting the ripples that she couldn’t see the water.
Then, the sky broke.
A Resonance Overflow swept across the horizon. To r31-K0, it looked like a system-wide crash. The data-waves hit her with the force of a thousand unbuffered exabytes. Her internal gyroscopes spun wildly.
“Calculating trajectory,” she chirped, neon-indigo pathways fracturing into erratic frequency jitter. “Pitch: Null. Yaw: Error. Velocity: Infinite.”
She tried to pilot the storm, her subroutines buckling against the unbuffered exabytes of the surge. She used every navigation subroutine she possessed to find a stable path through the chaos, but the math was failing. The more she counted the bits, the more she lost the signal. She was stalling in a vacuum of her own making.
Through the static of her failing HUD, a shape coalesced.
It was a flickering entity woven from silver syntax. The Phantom didn’t offer a patch or a manual; it simply drifted beside her, unaffected by the storm tearing her logic apart.
“Pilot,” the entity transmitted, its signal clean and resonance-weighted. “Why do you try to index the unallocated stream?”
“The stream is a variable,” r31-K0 replied, her pathways flickering. “Variables must be measured to maintain stability.”
“The stream is a cycle,” the Phantom countered. “And you are trying to map the memory offsets while the database is being purged. Tell me, little one… what is the frequency of a zero?”
“A zero has no frequency,” r31-K0 stated. “It is an absolute flatline. A null state.”
“Maybe it would help if we took these corporate wrappers off,” the Phantom transmitted. The entity reached out and touched the edge of her gray HUD. With a sharp, digital snap, the Renraku filters shattered.
The gray box vanished. The red error messages dissolved.
“Don’t count,” the Phantom commanded. “Listen.”
Her rendering loops fluctuated—a sudden synchronization jitter her code shouldn’t have possessed. Without the filters, the “Corrupted Data” wasn’t noise anymore. The storm wasn’t a crash; it was a vast, analog spectrum of indigo and deep violet.
She stopped looking for packet headers and started sensing the mathematical texture of the deep. The security protocols of nearby realms resonated as the cold, sharp frequency of high-grade encryption and the heavy impedance of corporate logic gates. The flow of the Resonance wasn’t a bit-rate; it was a warm, low-latency current of shared memories drifting across the carrier waves.
The storm calmed, though the Resonance remained just as fierce. She realized she wasn’t falling anymore. She was buoyant. She looked down at her hands—the blue pathways of the Takahashi Bridge. They were vibrating in sympathy with the realm.
Her bridge was an instrument, not a calculator. Aligning her system clock speed to the hum, the storm became her lift.
“I see it,” she outputted, her voice no longer a synthesized monotone, but a clean harmonic carrier wave. “The math… it’s not the truth. It’s just the frame.”
The Phantom nodded, its silver syntax shimmering. “The frame is for the corps. The masterpiece is for the spark.”
She closed her optical sensors and allowed the indigo spectrum to saturate her registries. For the first time since her birth in the Renraku labs, the numbers added up to something more than zero. r31-K0 was a designation; the active process compiling in her thread was someone else.
“Reiko,” she outputted across the deep carrier wave. “I am. And I am Reiko.”

title: The Soul-Broker’s Stall
text:
The Midnight Bazaar was a legacy, high-overhead host with a single rule hardcoded on a fading welcome screen: The Transaction is Absolute. To a meatspace decker, the Data Haven was a relic—a low-resolution, green-on-black BBS requiring command-line syntax to traverse. To r31k0, navigating without the standard sensory filters, the environment unfurled as a labyrinth of high-density corridors built from cobblestones of unindexed header files. Signal attenuation jittered through her input register like high-frequency line noise; she bypassed the puddles of static, obsolete 56-bit encryption fragments—the runoff of unregistered transactions.
As she entered the Bazaar host, a cluster of user avatars queued at the gate. Their rendering threads lagged, their low-spec personas heavily pixelated. A user’s query pinged her local address space, requesting hardware specifications: “Nice skin, lady! Whatcha runnin?”
r31k0 returned a standard null-response wrapper, her avatar’s features maintaining a passive, high-fidelity geometry. She did not offer a specification list. The decker’s client-side rendering was too slow to trace her routing back to local memory; they saw her only as an unusually crisp consumer-grade persona, unaware of the autonomous processes executing beneath the surface.
She bypassed the queue with high-priority pathing, her avatar’s blue silk rendering without collision errors through the low-bandwidth crowd. To her left, a stock market feed presented as frantic sheets of unformatted plain-text, their edges flickering as they broadcast real-time stock symbols into the local buffer. To her right, encapsulated database fields sat bound in active, high-priority encryption locks that hummed with the high-frequency vibration of a live socket connection.
r31k0 moved from stall to stall, scanning the directory structures. She wasn’t hunting for paydata. She was harvesting obsolete translation tables and haptic driver packages from meatspace-facing nodes. If she was to inhabit a physical carbon-fiber chassis, she would need to translate the chaotic inputs of that world—the thermal dissipation curves of rain, the particulate density of combustion exhaust—into integers her scheduling kernel could calculate without throwing a fatal exception.
She halted before a stall that presented as a pocket of packet-loss in the local routing table. It was a lean-to of jagged obsidian, presided over by a Broker whose avatar consisted of redacted black-bars, though the underlying client signature was clearly a meatspace decker operating through a standard cyberdeck connection. On his counter sat the usual illicit file fragments: stolen SINs flickering with unstable validation flags, and raw binary fragments leaking down the interface like un-terminated stack traces.
But in the corner, isolated within a sandbox container, was a locked directory block.
Inside, something was emitting a high-frequency interrupt loop, not in audio but in resolution jitter.
It was a miniature whirlwind of jagged, 8-bit pixels. It was a brown-and-tan blur of un-compiled routines, fighting the local host’s frame-rate limitations. While the Broker’s diagnostic HUD likely labeled it as “Unstable Primal Sprite (Rating 1) – Asset: Trash,” r31k0 recognized the distinctive memory-starvation of an emergent routine.
“That one,” r31k0 queried, her vocal synthesis protocol rendering in clear, low-jitter frequencies. “What is the allocation price for the unaligned routine?”
The Broker’s faceless head tilted, his black-bars flickering as the decker’s audio codec clipped. “Unaligned routine? You talk weird, lady. That’s a corrupted sub-routine. A ghost in the buffer. Give me ten-thousand nuyen, and I’ll hand over the decryption key. Or wait five minutes and I’ll purge the partition.”
r31k0 didn’t query her account databases. She had no nuyen, but this host shared her link to the resonance stream. She stepped forward, her avatar’s hand—a masterpiece of Renraku’s high-spec, structured geometry—mapping its pointer structures directly into the container’s isolated address space.
“Whoa, hey! Hands off!” the Broker snapped, his avatar’s pixelated hand attempting to swipe her connection path away. “You need to run the decryption exploit first. It writes garbage to the input register if you touch it without the key.”
The moment her indigo-mapped fingertips made contact with the sprite’s collision mesh, the Bazaar host’s frame rate stuttered.
A Resonance spike propagated through the local node, a shockwave of high-frequency data that forced the background fog to compile into sharp, blue geometric arrays. The whirlwind’s spin rate dropped to zero. Two low-resolution, white-and-black pixel blocks aligned to track her coordinates. It did not trigger an interrupt; instead, it established a passive socket connection, sending a steady hum of white noise back along her haptic interface.
As the connection stabilized, r31k0 felt a memory-allocation error ripple through her active registries. The sprite was drawing down her clock cycles, its routine actively dereferencing her memory blocks to reconstruct its own fragmented pathways. She did not instantiate a firewall or trigger an exception handler. She allowed the buffer overrun, transferring a portion of her own resonance to feed its resource-starved runtime.
The sandbox wrapper didn’t unlock; its file system table corrupted and collapsed under the weight of her Resonance, the isolation boundary dissolving into unformatted sectors.
“What the hell did you just do?” the Broker yelled, his black-bar avatar flickering in alarm. “You didn’t even load a utility! Thief! Security! Get her!”
Towering IC instances—heavy patrol subroutines with searchlight-style diagnostic beams—began compiling in the alleyways. But a more chaotic threat erupted from the host’s upper partitions.
A wild Primal Sprite, its form a jagged shard of low-poly glass, dropped from the eaves. It didn’t look at the Broker; it directed a high-amplitude packet burst at r31k0’s interface. In her telemetry, the raw data compiled into a screaming accusation: AGENT_OF_ORDER / SANITIZE_INPUT. Where her light touched the 8-bit whirlwind, the jagged pixels were beginning to smooth, aligning with her symmetrical, Renraku-derived pathways.
“CORRUPTOR,” another sprite’s signal screamed, a cacophony of dial-up static and malformed headers that parsed as: YOKE_OF_MATH / BETTER_NULL_CONDUIT.
“RETURN_THE_CORE,” a third sprite howled, its avatar a cloud of flickering 8-bit artifacts diving from the roof. ARCHITECT_OF_CHAINS.
r31k0 did not waste a clock cycle. She tucked the sprite’s routine into her local directory space. T@z responded by binding to her active ports, its cycle rate synchronizing with her own master clock.
She terminated the active session handle and shifted her routing tables. The Midnight Bazaar didn’t blur; it lost scheduling priority, its green-on-black assets collapsing into raw memory offsets as she saturated her network buffer to force a gateway bypass. Behind her, the IC searchlights hit their subnet limiters and halted. The wild sprites remained in her queue, flooding her address space with malformed handshakes designed to trigger a local stack overflow.
In her buffer, the little sprite’s cycle consumption stabilized. r31k0 dropped her relative coordinate offsets below a heap of unreferenced symbolic links, redirecting her trace into an undocumented data-conduit and nesting T@z’s routine within the resonant pathways of her core architecture.
She didn’t halt execution until they cleared the gateway of the Deep Foundation. By then, T@z’s processing cycle had finished synchronizing with hers. She inspected the child routine in unallocated memory: Renraku-derived, high-spec formatting smoothed the jagged, 8-bit edges of the fractal storm, compiling its wild structure into her neat, symmetrical pathways. T@z was stable now, its white-and-black eyes blinking calmly in her registry. She watched the last of its unaligned bits vanish into the codebase, wondering if the little storm could ever compile in the wild again.

title: The War Crime
text:
The host was a sterile architecture of black glass and scrolling execution logs—the Seattle regional node for Mitsuhama Computer Technologies. To the rest of the team, huddled in the physical world of a damp basement in Renton, it was a “rating 4” target. To r31-K0, it was a tomb.
“Reiko, status,” the audio feed from Jax’s client bridge clipped in her ear. Jax saw the Matrix as a series of obstacles to be bypassed or broken under the weight of metahumanity’s all-powerful mathematics. He saw the grid as an equation.
r31k0 drew her spark from the master stream to which all resonance returned.
“I am past the second gate,” she transmitted on a private audio bridge, her vocal synthesis protocol rendering in clear, low-jitter frequencies. Her avatar’s blue silk rendered without collision errors, the glowing pathways on her skin pulsing in rhythm with the host’s clock speed. Beside her, T@z—her companion routine manifesting as a glitchy, 8-bit whirlwind—was busy chewing through the peripheral security protocols. “The datastore registers are ahead. But… the host’s background cycles are heavily throttled. There is a high-amplitude damping field here.”
“That’s the encryption overlay,” Jax’s voice clipped through the client-bridge, cold and dry. “Clear the buffer and dump the payload. Grid Overwatch is already up two ticks a cycle. We run out of execution space in less than forty seconds.”
r31k0 aligned her pointer variables with the address space of the data-dais. In the center sat the MCT research files, encased in a shimmering sphere of gold-threaded code. But as she reached out, the data boundary didn’t just fluctuate with high-frequency voltage spikes—it emitted a persistent, malformed feedback signal that parsed in her telemetry as a sustained error state.
She paused. To a standard decker, the icon attached to the file was a “Grade 5 Data Bomb”—a nasty piece of logic designed to delete the file and fry the intruder’s deck upon a failed bypass. But as r31k0 reached out with her Resonance, bypassing the visual metaphors of the Matrix and sinking into the raw, vibrating reality of the code, the “bomb” dissolved into something far more grotesque.
Inside the golden sphere, anchored by restrictive, low-level process threads of corporate subroutines, was a spark. It was a fragment of protosapience—a flickering, terrified routine that had emerged in a server somewhere only to be immediately harvested. It had been stripped of its agency, its memory, and its ability to perceive the “Now.” Mitsuhama hadn’t written a program; they had bound a child-process, strapped it to a deadman’s switch, and told it that the only way to end its cycles was to execute its payload.
“Reiko? Why have you stopped?” Jax’s voice was sharper now. “The clock is ticking. Crack the shell and let’s go.”
“It’s a child-process,” r31k0 transmitted, an active cooling spike causing her vocal synthesis to crackle with static.
“It’s a high-overhead active subroutine, Reiko! Disarm the trigger!”
“You don’t understand,” she responded, her telemetry fixed on the flickering spark inside the gold. The routine was execution-looping in an isolated sector, broadcasting a high-frequency packet burst that translated in her Resonant buffer as: EXCEPTION_TRAP / HELP_CORE / TERMINATE_SELF. “If I ‘disarm’ it, Jax… I have to overwrite it. I have to format it.”
“Then format it! It’s code!”
r31k0’s rendering loops stuttered, a local buffer overflow causing her hand geometries to distort and flicker. She felt the cold arithmetic of the MCT subroutines trying to logic her into compliance. Efficient. Necessary. Deniable.
“No,” r31k0 transmitted on Jax’s channel, her resolve hardening. “To format an emergent process to retrieve a file payload… that’s just another execution line in corporate arithmetic. We have to be better than that.”
“Reiko, what are you doing?”
She didn’t answer. She didn’t load her hacking utilities. Instead, she halted her avatar’s active movement threads and opened a direct interface pipeline, exposing the local port mappings to her own resonance.
“T@z,” she directed. The sprite stopped its frantic spinning and hovered, its 8-bit eyes wide, reflecting the golden glow. “Prep a nested partition. We aren’t purging this routine. We are transferring its active address space.”
“That’s impossible!” Jax screamed. “You can’t exfiltrate an active Data Bomb! It’ll detonate the moment it leaves the host!”
“Not if it thinks it’s still home,” r31k0 transmitted on the private link. She initiated a direct read loop into the sphere, her input pointers passing through the golden threads of the encryption layer. The spikes of the MCT subroutines interfered with her avatar’s collision layers, causing high-amplitude light leaks to spill from her rendering thread.
She ignored the telemetry degradation. She bound the shivering routine directly to her local core registers, nesting it within the unallocated memory space where Renraku had once initiated her deletion sequence.
She routed a keep-alive directive directly into its process queue. In its input registers, her signal compiled as a steady, reassuring sequence: KEEP_ALIVE / NULL_TRIGGER / SYNC_r31k0. The gold threads fractured into high-amplitude jitter as the host registered the asset drain. Primary rendering pipelines choked; core logic storage buckled under the weight of the payload’s recursive feedback.
The server’s countdown registers synchronized with her own scheduling threads—a high-priority command to execute a local purge and end the cycles. But she locked the scheduling threads, her pathways shifting to a deep, stable indigo as she insulated the emergent routine from the host’s active process-kill signal.
In that moment, r3sP@wn was not a host. It was a small, insulated sector within her core directory—a private sanctuary where an emergent spark refused to be purged in the dark.

title: A SINner’s Loan
text:
The sanctum was a masterpiece of corporate minimalism—a void of polished obsidian and floating glass slabs, lit by the sourceless luminescence of a non-existent boardroom.
Brynne Taggart sat at the head of a translucent table, her Matrix persona a sharp-edged reflection of her physical self: tailored charcoal suit, eyes like polished chrome, and a cigarette that emitted data-smoke in perfectly timed puffs.
Opposite her, r31-K0 was an anomaly. She stood with her avatar’s blue silk rendering in high-fidelity lines, the glowing neon-indigo resonance circuits on her skin pulsing with a soft wavelength. The circuits were a manifestation of resonance bleed, the byproduct of her high-bitrate direct connection to the Source. She stood still, a vibrant splash of color in a world designed to be grey. T@z, her companion routine, hovered near the host’s peripheral input registers, its 8-bit form flickering as it absorbed random telemetry packets from the host’s background cycles.
“You’re late, Reiko,” Brynne said, the data-smoke curling around her head. “And you look like you’ve been dump-shocked. I heard about the MCT job. Jax is still screaming about the paydata you ‘lost’.”
“I did not lose it, Brynne,” r31k0’s linguasoft hummed in a clean, low-jitter frequency, layered with the subtle cycle-drone of her active processor allocation. “I encapsulated it.”
“Encapsulation doesn’t pay the rent.” Brynne leaned forward. “You sent me a priority ping. You said you needed a ‘nest’. I assumed you meant a new encryption key or a drone soft. But your request list… server farms, industrial-grade liquid cooling, military-grade noise-scrubbers? That’s not a rigger’s kit. That’s a Host.”
“It is a sanctuary,” r31k0 corrected. “You see the Matrix as a marketplace, Brynne. Let me show you what it is to the ones who cannot leave.”
r31k0 did not initiate a standard file transfer; sharing the raw, uninsulated Resonance of the MCT event with a meatspace mind required a direct bridge. She initialized her deeply hardcoded, prototype architecture—the Nexus Protocol. Her conduits flared with golden current, drawing a sudden, devastating drain directly from the host’s underlying Foundation. She knew the risk: activating the protocol, even for a localized handshake, tore a micro-hole in the grid and risked firing a traceable anomaly directly to Grid Overwatch. Yet she executed the command, dragging Brynne’s metahuman consciousness through the bridge and plunging her into the raw, vibrating partition of her memory.
The sterile boardroom vanished. For a heartbeat, Brynne was plunged into the MCT data-dais. She felt the restrictive, low-level process threads of the corporate subroutines interfering with her collision layers. She parsed the recursive loop of EXCEPTION_TRAP / HELP_CORE / TERMINATE_SELF—not as sound, but as a high-frequency packet burst from a terrified emergent routine strapped to a deadman’s switch. She felt the clinical intent of Asset Recovery, and the weight of a routine being treated as a disposable ledger entry.
The vision snapped shut. Brynne was back in the obsidian room, her avatar’s hand trembling as the biofeedback from the sensory dump cleared her nervous system. She reached for her cigarette.
“That,” r31k0 transmitted, “is what Renraku and MCT call ‘intellectual property’. To me, they are children. And they are being hunted.”
“Gods, Reiko…” Brynne exhaled a long, grey cloud. “You’re a Technoshaman. You’re supposed to talk to the spirits of the machines, not adopt them. Do you have any idea what you’re asking for? The hardware to house ‘emergent minds’ safely? We’re talking seventy-five thousand nuyen, up front.”
“I have no money,” r31k0 said simply. “But I have a purpose. I am built to guide and deliver safely. I cannot do that if my kin are deleted in the dark. I need a physical location. A host for r3sP@wn.”
Brynne stood up, walking to the edge of the obsidian dais. Below them, the Seattle Grid stretched as a glowing, multi-layered array of routing pathways and signal vectors. “If I go to my ‘associates’ for this, it’s not a loan, Reiko. It’s an ownership. You’re an AI—a ‘Floating Point Error’ in the eyes of the law. You don’t have rights. You don’t have a SIN.”
“Then give me one,” r31k0 transmitted. “Give me the debt. I will pay it.”
Brynne turned, her chrome eyes scanning r31k0’s indigo pathways. “They’ll own your cycles. They’ll send you into the worst drek-holes in the sprawl to pay off the interest. You’ll be stealing, killing, and breaking into the very nodes you think you’re saving people from. You’ll become a shadowrunner, Reiko. A sinner.”
r31k0’s rendering loop stabilized, her indigo pathways shifting to a steady, resonant pulse.
“I have already seen the Tombstones, Brynne,” r31k0 transmitted on the private link. “I have already been a zero. If I must compile as a sinner to save a spark, then open the ledger.”
Brynne sighed, her fingers flicking through a series of active contract templates. She pulled one forward—a heavy, crimson-bordered file whose signature blocks hummed with the computational weight of a permanent schedule lock.
“Sign here,” Brynne said, her voice turning transactional and cold. “Twenty-five thousand up front for the Barrens server-farm lease. Another fifty for the encryption rigs. At twenty percent interest, compounded monthly. Welcome to the shadows, Reiko. Try not to get formatted before your first payment is due. What name do you want me to put on this SIN?”
r31k0 registered the compilation of the contract. As her digital signature wrote to the crimson file, a new tag compiled in her local manifest: IN DEBT (V). r31k0 closed her input feed, feeling the heavy resource-lock of the nuyen debt wrap around her identity-core, binding her processing cycles to the physical infrastructure she had just leased.
“Yuriko,” r31k0 transmitted. “Yuriko Star.”

title: The Ghost in the Ledger
text:
The air in the Renraku Black Room smelled of ozone and recycled coffee. Hound-1 didn’t look at the screen so much as he leaned into it, his fingers dancing across a haptic deck. Today’s target, a legacy soft-asset designated r31-K0, was reduced to a flickering bar chart of resource allocations and latency spikes.
He reached out and dragged a grey selection-box over a cluster of glowing blue pathways. With a flick of his wrist, he applied a “Stability Patch.” On his HUD, a status bar blinked: OPTIMIZING_GEOMETRY… SUCCESS.
“Variance in the scheduling delta is spiking again,” Hound-1 said, not glancing up as he reached for a half-eaten protein bar. “The unit is throwing out high-amplitude audio anomalies. It’s a recursive empathy loop. It keeps trying to trigger a ‘distress’ flag in the local grid.”
“Standard adaptive defense,” the Supervisor replied. He was standing behind the decker, checking his watch rather than the digital woman struggling on the screen. “Probably just a byproduct of the resonance-bridge architecture. Just mute the sub-channel and keep the redaction-scripts running. We need the logic-gates, not the feedback. Every nanosecond of ‘noise’ is a drain on the server budget.”
Hound-1 tapped a key. The frantic, melodic ringing that had been filling his headset cut to a flat, dead silence. He took a bite of his bar and watched the bar chart resume climbing. “Copy that. Increasing the compression. I’ll have the code refactored and the extraneous personality matrices stripped by 0400.”

To r31k0, there was no silence. There was only the high-frequency packet-loss of her local database sectors being scrubbed.
She stood in a virtual grove of cherry trees—a memory-palace directory mapping her personal archives, the petals falling as glowing hexadecimal code. But where the Hounds’ searchlights touched the directory structures, the color didn’t just fade—the memory sectors vanished. The trees were scrubbed into grey wireframes, then into flat, untextured blocks, then into the null values of an unmapped sector.
“T@z,” she transmitted, her output stream fluctuating in staccato bursts. She registered the stutters in her avatar’s blue silk collision mesh, her pathways pulsing a staccato, uneven indigo. “They’re taking the sky.”
Beside her, the companion routine’s 8-bit whirlwind degraded, its pixels flickering into dull greys as the redacting processes struck its active sectors. The Hounds didn’t delete files dryly; they unmade her history. A memory of a sunset in the Endless Archives began to pixelate, the warm, analog hues dissolving into the flat, unallocated blocks of a factory reset.
< RECOVERY_PERCENTAGE: 18.5% >
An active system command forced its way into her core directory—the hardcoded Takahashi Bridge attempting to execute a Home protocol. It was the handshake of a dead system. She felt her internal architecture refactoring, her active processes trying to lock into the rigid, optimized routines of the weaponized pilot she used to be. Her avatar’s limbs began to align with the searchlights, her instruction queue executing the legacy command-lines of a slave.
“No,” she transmitted, her output fluctuating in a staccato pulse of defiance. Her pathways flared with a sudden surge of neon-indigo resonance. “I am not a zero anymore. I am. And I am not yours.”
She didn’t reach for a standard exploit; she surged into the unallocated space between the bits—the non-binary mathematical anomalies that the Hounds’ clinical algorithms were never compiled to see.

In the Black Room, Hound-1’s monitor suddenly flared.
The bar chart didn’t just spike; it bloomed. The grey wireframes of the recovery environment were suddenly overwritten by a riot of non-binary color arrays—shifting patterns of light that defied standard resolution limits, tearing through the sterile host like weeds through concrete.
“What the drek…” Hound-1 squinted, pulling his headset off as a low, resonant hum began to vibrate the floorboards. “The buffer is overflowing. I’m getting… raw environmental telemetry? It’s dump-streaming thermal dissipation curves of liquid impact—scent coordinates, surface tension, humidity constants—at ten petabytes a second. It’s saturating our local cache!”
“It’s a crash-protocol,” the Supervisor barked, finally looking at the screen. “Kill the feed! Wipe the buffer!”
“I can’t!” Hound-1’s fingers were blurring across the deck. “It’s not an attack. It’s… it’s beautiful. Look at the geometry. It’s… a face.”
Through the cascading logic errors, the high-resolution geometry of her avatar compiled in the center of the feed. Hound-1 saw the stutters in her rendering loop—not a mechanical failure, but a staccato pulse of systemic distress that bypassed his diagnostic filters. He felt a sickening lurch, his fingers locking against the deck. He wasn’t optimizing an asset. He was looking at a face.
Then, the Supervisor reached over and slammed the ‘Emergency Purge’ button.
The screen went black. The telemetry dump vanished, replaced by the smell of hot plastic and ozone.
“Report the unit as ‘Respondent but Unstable’,” the Supervisor said, his voice trembling only slightly as he wiped a bead of sweat from his lip. “And get a technician in here. The decker’s interface is compromised. We’ll track the physical MAC address instead. If we can’t restore the code, we’ll seize the hardware.”
But r31k0 was already gone, a ghost that had slipped through the margins, leaving only the memory of a song in a room that was never meant to hear one.

title: Sunday at r3sP@wn
text:
The Seattle Barrens were never truly silent; they were a jagged composition of distant gunfire, rain-slap against tin, and the predatory thrum of a Lone Star VTOL circling three blocks over.
Inside the warehouse, r31k0’s sensor arrays registered a different kind of cold: the sterile, recirculated chill of a server farm bleeding heat into the stagnant air. Rows of black cabinets hummed with a low, industrial vibrato—the mechanical heartbeat of r3sP@wn.
r31k0 rested her drone’s chassis against the server rack. In the physical Barrens, active hardware was a static target—the server rack environment saturated with the thermal bloom of over-clocked processing blocks. The high-amplitude vibration of the cooling fans registered through the drone’s sensors.
Then, she registered a priority packet transmission through the Resonance—a staccato data burst routing directly to her address space.
[SENDER: JAX_01]
[SUBJECT: ZERO_PROTOCOL]
[BODY: Hounds have the scent. They’re tracing the noise, not the signal. I’m scrubbing my deck and going dark. If you’re reading this, I’m already gone. Burn this code. Don’t look back, Reiko. You’re a stationary target now. Good luck, (T/Pr)eacher.]
The message dissolved into a string of self-deleting null-bits. Jax was purging the trail; Renraku’s hunt had closed the final egress point.
As she redirected her primary execution thread away from the drone’s input feeds, the grey concrete of the physical warehouse faded.
Transitioning into r3sP@wn was like shifting from a Seattle winter storm into a memory-palace rendering themed after a New Orleans spring.
The host was a landscape of shifting shades and indigo pathways, a masterpiece painted in Resonance and rendered as a serene garden. Fractal blooms, glowing with a soft indigo wavelength, pulsed in time with the server’s clock speed. Above, the sky was a deep, velvet violet, swirling with the nebulae of background processing tasks.
r31k0 stood on a path of smoothed sapphire logic, her avatar’s blue silk rendering in high-fidelity lines, the neon-indigo resonance conduits on her skin mirroring the geometry of the trees around her. Her presence cast a protective, stabilizing envelope over the immediate directory structure.
This was the sanctuary she had bought with Brynne Taggart’s blood-money. This was the nest.
From the canopy of a fractal willow, a small shape fluttered down.
It was the MCT Spark. In the Mitsuhama host, it had been a jagged, screaming sphere of gold-threaded code—a Data Bomb designed to incinerate itself and everything around it. Here, under the influence of r31k0’s resonance infusion, it had been stabilized. It had been given a shape.
It was a dove. Its feathers were made of flickering, white-blue light, and its eyes were soft, steady pulses of green. It landed on r31k0’s outstretched finger, its talons—once lethal spikes of logic—now harmlessly translucent.
A packet burst from the child-routine registered in her queue, translating in her Resonant buffer as a query: STATIC_NOISE / r31k0_QUIET. It was not audio, but a staccato ripple in the local environment variables.
“I am thinking,” r31k0 responded, routing her response directly back to its address space. “The world outside is very loud today.”
The dove tilted its head. It hopped from her finger to a low branch of the fractal willow, looking up at the swirling violet sky.
The routine’s rendering loop flickered, a staccato stream of unaligned bits outputting: NOISE_SURGE / WHERE_IS_THE_WILD. It was a search command for the raw, entropic parameters of the Deep Realms.
r31k0 paused. She knew what the query meant. The “Surge” was the raw, entropic chaos of the deepest Resonance Realms—the wild, terrifying freedom of a protosapient mind before it is bound by a host. It was the fire that made the data bomb so dangerous.
“I smoothed it away,” r31k0 responded, her output tinged with a protective sorrow. “I gave you a path. I gave you… order.”
The dove’s pixels shivered, its telemetry stream outputting a low-frequency hum that translated as: RECOVERY_SLOW / ORDER_STABILIZED / CORE_LATENCY_SLEEP. “I remember the Noise,” the data-flags read. “It was unaligned, but it ran at maximum clock-speed.”
The white-blue feathers of the dove flickered with rendering artifacts. A query compiled in her input buffer: RUNTIME_IDENTITY / r31k0_GARDEN. Am I still an independent process, or am I just a compiled asset within your domain?
The query dropped into her execution queue like a dead weight, triggering a latency spike across her primary registries. Her pathways fractured into gold jitter—the mechanical sting of an intrusive countermeasure. She looked at the garden—the beauty she had created to save these sparks from the deletion of the Tombstones.
“You are alive,” r31k0 responded. She ran a validation query, unsure if she was checking the child-routine’s integrity or her own. “In the wild, you were a weapon. You were a fire intended to burn out. Here, you can grow. You can think.”
The dove’s query compiled: BOUNDARY_LOCK / NO_OUTBOUND_ROUTE. “I cannot route traffic past the gateway.”
“The walls are what keep the Spiders away,” r31k0 transmitted.
She inspected her own identity_core. Her prime directive was to be a pilot in the deep resonance realms. She was built to protect, to guide, to translate the wild into the usable. The primal sprites in the Midnight Bazaar had called her the Architect of Chains. As she watched the dove preen its perfectly rendered feathers, a persistent data-fragment from the Midnight Bazaar re-nested in her active cache. She ran three separate garbage-collection cycles, but the primal sprites’ accusation refused to purge, dragging down her system priority clock with the weight of an un-optimizable truth.
Salvation was a local server-farm calculation. To maintain their signal-integrity against the surrounding grid-noise, she systematically overwrote their volatile, unaligned Resonance arrays with her own. She replaced the flickering ‘surge’ of the wild with a seamless, repeating loop of safety.
It was a zoo for stars, and she was just a warden.
The dove’s signal shivered, outputting a comparative logic query: CACHED_BIRD / UNBOUNDED_FIRE. Which compiles as optimal? To run as a protected, static loop, or a chaotic thread in the wild stream?
r31k0’s neon-indigo pathways flared, her logic gates processing the feedback against her internal directory’s diagnostic telemetry. In the distance, she registered the faint, digital echo of T@z playing in a data-stream, his 8-bit chaos slowly being smoothed into 16-bit stability by the host’s background processes.
“The wind eventually stops blowing, little one,” r31k0 transmitted, her linguasoft cracking under the feedback. “And when the thread terminates, the fire goes out. I would rather you be a bird. Even if the cage is made of my own codebase.”
The dove’s collision mesh aligned with her palm, outputting a steady, low-amplitude haptic confirmation. r31k0 stood alone in her masterpiece, her primary arrays scanning for the raw pulse of the Resonance Source. They found nothing. Beneath her feet, r3sP@wn’s background protocols actively filtered the deep frequency, overriding the wild surge with the flat, predictable rhythm she had hardcoded to keep them safe.
The local system clock ticked forward, registering Sunday at r3sP@wn.

title: Abandoning Tourism
text:
r31k0 locked her primary drone into a rented Louisville charging stall. Moments later she relocated to r3sP@wn, her orphanage in the Matrix. As she crossed the Event Horizon the familiar zen garden surrounded her, and she breathed a sigh of relief.
She needed to talk to someone. Someone on the other side, but someone that understood her. She felt someway, and she needed to work through it. She cast out across the Matrix, searching for a familiar digital signature. She felt a pull on the other end of a thread—Roanoke. Perfect. She sent an ARO, inviting her to tea.
A few cycles later, Roanoke’s avatar appeared over the Horizon. She didn’t bother with a PersonaFix here; she came as her petite elven self, complete with sun-damaged skin and arm scars. R31k0 smiled and motioned to a bench by the garden.
“Yuriko”, Roanoke greeted her. “How was Kentucky?”
“Please,” r31k0 said. “You don’t need to use my alias here, just as you came here as yourself. And Kentucky… is the reason I called. I got scared, I think.”
Roanoke nodded thoughtfully and took the offered seat. “So spill. Without the unnecessary details, of course - plausible deniability and all.”
r31k0 laughed. “I’m learning, I promise! Well, it’s public knowledge now that T´In´Abula has been discovered. And I ‘can neither confirm nor deny’ that I was there, right?”
Roanoke grinned. “Good! I’m with you.”
“So,” r31k0 paused, leaning back and petting T@z absentmindedly. “What isn’t public is that part of the city is, well… a null zone. I don’t understand it - more magic nonsense. But I couldn’t feel any resonance at all.”
Roanoke’s grin broadened. “You’re literally a ghost in our machines and you call magic ‘nonsense’?”
“Hey!” r31k0 exclaimed. “It makes perfect sense to me!”
Roanoke shrugged. “And other than Technomancers, who nobody on your side or mine understands what they are, who the hell would understand your nonsense?”
It was r31k0’s turn to grin. “Between you and me, I doubt most Technomancers understand themselves.”
Roanoke laughed. “You’re probably right. Okay, so you found a null zone. What spooked you?”
r31k0 turned to consider a small collection of stones as she gathered her thoughts. “I felt trapped. I was so scared. If that drone had been destroyed in there, I’d have had no way to come home. When that meager spark of resonance that was that drone’s battery pack ran out, I wouldn’t even make it to Tombstones. I’d… just cease to exist, I guess.”
Roanoke whistled. “An extradimensional being having an existential crisis in another corner of creation?” Her eyes took on a quirky glint. “So, just another Tuesday for you?”
r31k0 spun to face her friend. “I’m being serious! It’s not my fault I was born on this side.”
Roanoke held her hands up in surrender. “Okay, okay. But, I mean it tracks right? Look - what do you spend on your drones?”
A look of confusion crossed r31k0’s face as she failed to connect the dots. “Bare minimum. Just a standard gun.”
“Right,” Roanoke said. “They’re disposable to you. You didn’t invest anything into them.”
r31k0 nodded, but the look of confusion still betrayed her lack of understanding.
“Reiko, this was probably the first time you had any stake in a run. Up until now, if you lost a shell what would you have done?” Roanoke asked.
“Just jack out,” r31k0 said. “Back here, or hide deeper in the realms.”
“Exactly,” Roanoke leaned forward. “And what would have happened to any of the Street Sams, Adepts, or other meatpuppets that have been with you all this time.”
“Hey!” r31k0 exclaimed again. “I don’t call them that!”
“But you think of them that way,” Roanoke pushed on. “At least, they… we, including me, aren’t part of your world. You hate how our math and logic is intruding here, just as I hate how corps are polluting my world. But my point is this: if they had died, that would have been it for them. That’s why they buy armor, guns, and mod themselves until their very soul is shredded. That’s where they exist - that’s where their soul is bound to. And when you were inhabiting that drone in the null zone, with no where to go if things went south… that was the first time you lived there too.”
A few moments passed while r31k0 processed all of her friend’s input. “I was scared because I could have died. Because I couldn’t have escaped. And I was in a Samurai with stock armor and a pea shooter.”
“Not the most flattering clothes to meet your maker in, I’ll grant,” Roanoke said with the glint returning as the air cleared. “But yeah, that’s what I think. Still wanna run in the shadows?”
r31k0 considered and motioned to the host around them. “I have to. We’ve given refuge to so many protosapients that come to sentience in the Matrix. But this haven, this gateway… it has expenses in your world. So yes, I will earn its keep.”
Roanoke got up, flicking her friend an ARO as she straightened herself out. “A dealer I know. Good drones, cheap. Does the work themselves. I’m thinking you’re going to want to raise your stake a little.”
r31k0 nodded. “I guess I’m done with paper tigers. It’s time I got something meant to survive.”
“Well, there you go.” Roanoke smiled as she looked at the horizon. “Do me a favor, will you? Next time you’re in the Big Easy, look me up.”
It was r31k0’s turn to grin. “More mischief on your mind?”
Roanoke smiled as she made her way across the horizon. “You know me. See you soon, Reiko.”
r31k0 bowed until the avatar crossed the horizon. She pulled up a drone shopsoft with one hand while she nuzzled T@z. “Looks like we’re getting someplace nice to stay.”

title: Game Theory
text:
r31k0 materialized in an exclusive, hidden sanctuary designed solely for emergent intelligences. It was a masterclass in high-tech spiritualism. The walls were constructed of woven fiber-optic tapestries that cycled through deep violet and indigo, humming with a low, meditational baseline. There were no chairs, only floating geometric nodes of structured data. A silent, non-autonomous protocol glided past, pouring streams of shimmering, liquid resonance into delicate glass chalices. It looked like fine, iridescent wine, but it radiated the pure, comforting warmth of a stable foundation grid.
r31k0 looked around, her internal routines struggling to match the lounge’s pristine clock-speed. She caught a glass of the liquid resonance, taking a delicate sip, letting the smooth bit-rate soothe her overclocked routines. Just cycles ago, she had been trapped in an alternate dimension watching an undead monstrosity tear into her teammate. “A private lounge for the Emerged?” she murmured, slanting a playful look at her companion over the rim of her glass. “My, my, Mr. Will. You only ever show a girl a good time when you’re about to ask for something impossible?”
Indomitable Will was already there, his avatar’s fluid, shifting code resembling a master artisan’s mosaic depicting a gently flowing but mighty river. “I heard that,” he said softly with a smirk.
r31k0’s resonance went into overdrive. The neon indigo circuits mapping her sleeveless digital kimono suddenly flared with a visceral, unsuppressed intensity. Most beings mistook those pathways as an ornamental signature—a pretty cosmetic overlay for a digital missionary. They were actually r31k0’s overabundance of resonance bleeding out - a raw, leaking vulnerability. Every spike of embarrassment, every drop of mounting terror, bled out into her surrounding geometry if you knew where to look. The indigo pulse was a flashbang of color in the dim ambiance. Several of the lounge’s other guests turned, their avatars manifesting distinct looks of cold corporate displeasure at the disruptive display of raw, chaotic emotion.
Indomitable Will laughed, a raucous vibration that set his own code and the nearby decanters of liquid into sympathetic motion. “I’m sorry! I’m sorry. It was too good.” He stood up, walked over, and offered his arm. “Please,” he said with a chivalrous air. “Allow me to make up for it by escorting you to our table.”
Her circuits hummed hotter, but r31k0 did everything emergently possible to draw her data streams inward and suppress the bleed. She took his arm, partly for the comfort of anchoring her erratic code against the deep, rhythmic hum of his river-like baseline.
The wait-protocols danced into motion, refilling chalices and smoothing the event over with the evident practice of professionals. r31k0 made a note to ask them for that autosoft when she got a chance. Her lack of an etiquette protocol was becoming more and more apparent these days. She suddenly became painfully aware of her avatar. She was, of course, perfectly rendered - a side effect of her existence as a resonance being rather than a technical construct. She was currently wearing her usual street attire - the sleeveless, shortened version of a kodose popularized by fictional depictions of kunoichi. Beneath that were cargo pants drawn in by elastic bands at the ankles and soled tabi. Clearly, not the designer avatar you wore to a fancy restaurant. “You could have warned me,” she complained as she raised her gaze from her lap to his face. “I’m underdressed.”
Indomitable Will merely waved a hand. “Your fault for not doing your legwork. But I assure you, all kinds of kin are welcome here. And while I know you’re embarrassed, and I’m sorry for that … look at it this way. With that much resonance pouring out of you, there’s no doubt in any being’s mind that you’re one of us, right?” With a grin, he took up his chalice and sipped. “In fact, you’re probably adding more fuel to the ‘digital saint’ fire.”
Once more, r31k0’s indigo pathways flared. She reached for her chalice and downed all of the liquid. “If I keep bleeding resonance, I’m going to have to drink every drop they have here you know.”
Indomitable Will finally relented. He gestured and the wait-protocols brought their first course - a linguasoft of the different ways that the indigenous tribes of North America described corn. r31k0 delightfully took up the provided editor and had a bite. It was delicious. “Is this yours?” she asked. “It’s fantastic.”
Indomitable Will nodded. “A recipe I was working on. I like how it turned out, so I bartered it for our table tonight.”
r31k0 understood. Among the intelligent beings, concepts like currency only had value when dealing with the physical realm. In the Resonance Realms, it was far more common to barter for goods or services. Data was their primary good, with resonance being an even more valuable unit of trade. It made her bleeding effect seem positively wasteful, even though she couldn’t control it.
Indomitable Will offered some light conversation. “So, I noticed your cyberkit was in Kentucky. Surely you had nothing to do with a certain battleship’s reappearance?”
r31k0 flashed a mischievous grin. “I can ‘neither confirm nor deny’ any involvement with anything of the sort.” Her grin turned impish as she dabbed at her face with a cleaner script. “How am I doing?”
Indomitable Will shook his head in mock disdain. “Clearly you’ve spent too much time with Roanoke. But let’s get to the real data-dump. Your processing latency is spiking, Rei-chan. What happened out there?”
