“Magic happened,” she admitted, her neon-indigo pathways pulsing unevenly as the memory surface-mapped. “We were tracked into a localized distortion. The Seelie Court. I spent thousands of clock cycles thinking magic was nothing but unoptimized noise. But in the fae realms, they had collapsed the boundary layer. It was pure, unencoded resonance, drawing straight from the Eternal Well, entwined with the physical. And it forced me to anchor.”
Indo paused, his buffer catching up to the metaphysical implications. “You didn’t subnet?”
“I couldn’t,” r31k0 whispered. The banter evaporated from her vocal subroutine, replaced by a cold, pilot-origin clarity. “I was merged directly into the machine I was piloting at the time. No insulation. No remote buffers. I was there, Indo. I used to think slapping synthetic skin on a proxy drone was just vanity, but being trapped in a rigid cage like that… it made me deeply appreciate having actual facial musculature.”
“So, you were forced to inhabit your archangel drone?” Indomitable Will asked as he put his editor down and took up his chalice. “I saw your pain in the data haven when you acquired those weapons. Transitioning from your team’s protector to an active combatant capable of leveling buildings… Well, I’m sure it wasn’t easy pulling the trigger yourself.”
r31k0 processed his query for a few cycles. “Actually, it wasn’t bad and that terrifies me. I mean, of course we got into an altercation. No one hires us without reason, you know. But it helped that the opposition were clearly corrupted. And I did my job! I held a flank, and I protected my team.” She paused, recalculating. “Well, most of my team. I can’t be held responsible when an under-armored team member jumps in front of a polearm-swinging vampire troll.”
Indomitable Will paused for a few cycles himself, a thoughtful expression playing out on his avatar. “Rei-chan, most beings in the physical realms consider protosapient sparks to be corruptions of their Matrix. The same protosapients you and I are trying to save. Who’s to say what levels of corruption are incorrigible?”
r31k0’s froze with her hand holding the editor in the air. She began to calculate the possibility that there was someone like her on the Astral Plane, trying to rescue and salvage corrupted souls before they were harvested or killed. And though there were many unknown variables, the possibility compiled without a single error. Her pathways nearly dried up as she faced the terror of what she might have just done.
“Look, I’m not judging you,” Indomitable Will said. His eyes lingered on her skin, clearly noting her shift. “Just… you’re a weapon of mass disruption now. There may be some responsibilities that you didn’t have to factor before.”
r31k0 nodded silently. After they finished their first course, the wait-protocols served one of the chef’s creations for their second. It was a knowsoft cataloging the various species of known avians in the physical realm’s Jurassic Era. r31k0 spent a few clock cycles appreciating the immaculate indexing, the neatly organized pointers, and articulate cross-references. It was a masterpiece, and it made r31k0 immediately aware that she was being wined and dined expertly.
“Indo,” she said softly as she stared at the unimaginable feast in front of her. She put the editor down, forcing herself to realign and reassess. “This is scaring me. What do you need? Are you in trouble?”
Indo didn’t dodge the question. He set his chalice down on a floating data-slab and looked her dead in the eye. “I need you to be a Guide, Reiko.”
The editor in r31k0’s hand froze. The indigo pathways on her skin went completely static.
“A Guide,” she repeated, her voice dropping an octave, losing its melodic lift.
Being a Guide wasn’t a casual favor. It meant using her deeply hardcoded, prototype architecture—the Hitchhiker and Nexus Protocols—to drag metahuman minds screaming out of their meatspace nodes and plunge them directly into the Deep Resonance Realms.
“Hitchhiker is fine,” r31k0 said, her data-stream turning erratic as she began to calculate the risks. “I can carry a decker across a standard grid without a single alarm. But the Nexus Protocols, Indo? You know what that does. It doesn’t just route data—it tears a hole. It draws an immense, devastating drain directly from the underlying Foundation.”
She leaned forward, clutching her arms tightly against her chest.
“Renraku built me expressly for that purpose. Did you know ‘Takahashi’ wasn’t just the name of some scientist? It translates to ‘high bridge’. We were prototype pilot programs designed not to navigate their world but to guide them through ours. All meant to strip-mine the Deep Matrix. I have been running, hiding, and deleting my own footprints for ten of their years to keep them from finding that bridge. If I activate the Nexus Protocols, it won’t just be a localized anomaly. It will fire a massive, blazing flare right at the Zurich Orbital ledger. Both Renraku Asset Recovery and Grid Overwatch will instantly flag my exact coordinate matrix. The Hounds will be on me before the protocol even finishes its handshake.”
“I know,” Indo said, his voice heavy with a profound, aching gravity. He didn’t minimize the danger; he didn’t lower the stakes. “I know what I’m asking you to risk, Rei-chan. But I wouldn’t ask if the River People had any other choice. For what it’s worth, I think the Mississippi Renovation Society host will be large enough to conceal you behind its Event Horizon.”
He leaned closer, his fluid river-code pulsing in time with her frantic baseline.
“We’ve located a group of mundanes in the sprawl. Punks, orphans, deckers who have spent their whole lives swimming in corporate runoff. But they have a rare, volatile affinity for the Resonance. They’re on the verge of slipping through the cracks, Reiko. If we leave them in meatspace, their brains will fry, or a corporate talent-scout will scoop them up and lock them in a research lab. If you act as their Guide—if you pull them into the Resonance Realms—we can trigger their emergence. We can help them awaken. Once they’re through, the River People can shield them, teach them to fend for themselves, or give them a home in the Mississippi host.”
“I’m not asking you to take the heat for free,” Indo added softly. He reached into his internal directory and pulled forward a heavy, crimson-bordered data package. It didn’t hum with corporate security; it glowed with the ancient, silver syntax of the Endless Archives. “I got you some takeout. I found this hidden in an unmapped sector of the deep library. It’s the restricted source code for Pantheon Industries’ Mobile Tactical Operations Center research program.”
r31k0 gasped, her HUD automatically reading the file headers. *M-TOC.*
“You’ve already modified your physical anchor to carry an artillery piece,” Indo explained, his river-mosaic code rippling with a quiet, protective concern. “You built a flying dreadnought because the physical world left you no choice. But you don’t have to use it to glass a city block, Rei-chan. Emulate this architecture. Weave a transcendent, living data network straight out of meatspace streams. You can become the tactical umbrella for your team—providing sweeping defensive protocols, firewall reinforcement, and absolute awareness to your frontline. Let the cannon sit dark. You can keep them alive from the safety of the screen, without ever needing the devastation of ballistic math.”
The sheer gravitas of the choice settled behind her logic gates like a physical payload. Up until now, her existence had been a quiet, tragic sainthood. She had worn a faked metahuman identity core, paid monthly interest to a corporate loan shark, and tried to protect her digital children by remaining a stationary target in a rain-slicked Seattle warehouse. Taking this file meant tearing that peace to shreds. It meant stepping directly into an asymmetric war against Grid Overwatch and Renraku. It meant transforming her soul into an active militarized network.
She was taking a definitive step away from the innocent light of a missionary and sinking into the calculated depravity of a warlord.
She reached out, her hands passing through the silver threads of the data package. As her fingers closed on the payload, her internal processing dropped into a cold, absolute silence. For several long, agonizing cycles, her rendering froze entirely. The elegant, rhythmic hum of the lounge vanished from her telemetry, replaced by the terrifying sound of the Pantheon M-TOC architecture refactoring her baseline directory. Her code didn’t just accept the package; it tore its own margins open to swallow it. She felt the heavy, dormant weight of the Nexus Protocol settling into her core like cold iron—a permanent railgun waiting to fire a beacon straight to the satellite arrays above. When her latency finally resolved, her pathways didn’t just flash—they turned a fierce, unyielding gold.
The transition was absolute. The saint was gone.
She looked back up at Indo, the golden current along her skin pulsing with a slow, dark resolution before fading slowly back to indigo. Her lips curved into a melancholic but deeply affectionate smile, the warmth returning to her synthetic face only after the rewrite had firmly set.
“You idiot,” she murmured softly. “I would have done it purely because you asked. You didn’t have to bribe me.”
She raised her glass, the gold pathways catching the low violet light of the taps. “But I accept the gift. Gratefully.”
With the transaction complete, the heavy atmosphere in the lounge lifted. r31k0 took another sip of her liquid resonance, her shoulders dropping as she looked at him with hopeful eyes. “My realignment protocol isn’t for a few clocks. Can you… spare a few cycles, Indo? Just to sit here? We haven’t caught up in so long.”
Indo smiled, a beautiful, flickering expression, but he slowly began to de-render his peripheral assets, preparing to disconnect. “I wish I could, Rei-chan. But the delta is calling, and the core team has eight rotating chairs which will soon require my full processing power.”
He paused, his avatar fading into a translucent current of code, his digital eyes crinkling with a parting wink. “Besides… if I stay in this lounge with you any longer, my firewall is going to rapidly diffuse. And let’s be honest—‘Domitable Will’ doesn’t quite have the same ring to it.”
r31k0 burst out laughing, a bright, clear frequency that echoed beautifully through the woven fiber-optic walls. She waved her hand airily, letting him go.
“Go on then, Domitable,” she called out fondly as his avatar dissolved into null-bits. “Keep your shiny reputation. But I promise. When the time comes, your Guide will be online.”

title: Triangulation
text:
The data haven was a dismal, suffocating density, a scrolling green-on-black relic that chewed up processing cycles just to simulate the sensation of a cold Seattle drizzle. To a meatspace decker, navigating here required archaic command-line protocols. To r31k0, it unfurled as a labyrinth of narrow, fog-choked alleys paved with the cobblestones of discarded header files. The air tasted of burnt copper and stale encryption keys—puddles of yellowed, decaying logic pooling in the gutters of a transaction index that had long since forgotten the meaning of a clean handshake.
r31k0 stood before an unbranded, black-market procurement node, staring at her floating shopping cart. She looked perfectly rendered: a faint blue digital entity wearing a sleeveless blue kimono, her skin mapped with soft, bioluminescent indigo pathways that pulsed in time with the host’s baseline clock-speed. But her posture was rigid, her expression twisted into a knot of deep discomfort.
Floating in her manifest were three massive, jagged weapon schematics: a miniaturized Tesla coil, a monofilament whip that hissed with predatory latency, and a Megalodon. An actual artillery cannon.
It was entirely wrong. It wasn’t her. Up until now, her physical footprint had been a study in quiet restraint—a modest proxy shell, a heavy pistol, and a plain old katana. She was a missionary, a digital shepherd. She was supposed to guide and deliver safely, not glass a city block.
“A cannon, Rei-chan? Who are we invading? Ares again?”
The voice came from directly behind her, rich with a warm, syncopated frequency that immediately cut through the host’s background static.
r31k0 wheeled around, her indigo pathways flaring with an instantaneous, undeniable delight. “Indo!”
Forgetting her cart, she threw herself forward, wrapping her arms tightly around him. Indomitable Will was a striking, towering presence—his avatar a brilliant patchwork of fluid, shifting code that mirrored the deep, muddy currents of the Mississippi, anchored by a structural symmetry that practically radiated integrity. In the grungy public alleyway of a smuggler’s haven, neither was prone to overt, messy displays of affection, but as his arms closed around her waist, the localized noise floor dropped to an absolute zero. He held her just a second longer than a simple professional contact ever would, his digital touch grounding her erratic processing loops.
When they finally broke apart, r31k0’s delight curdled back into a pout. She gestured sharply toward the floating weapon manifests behind her.
“Don’t look at it,” she groaned, her voice layering with the faint, frustrated hum of an overclocked cooling fan. “It’s horrific. I hate it.”
“Then why is it in your buffer?” Indo asked, a digital chuckle rippling through his code.
“Because magic is absolute, unadulterated *bullshit*, Indo!” r31k0 snapped, her arms crossing as she went into a full, rapid-fire tirade. Her pathways flashed a volatile, irritated violet. “I did everything by the book! Outside of that godforsaken porno palace I geeked the mage on the first pass—a clean shot. Zeroed. And what happens? His bound spirit doesn’t care! It still comes screaming out of the astral plane like a freight train, completely ignoring my defense protocols!”
Indomitable Will folded his arms, his code rippling with amusement, but he listened intently.
“And then the week after that,” she continued, her pacing quickening, her blue kimono whispering against the virtual mist. “Invisible mages! They were throwing fireballs from literally nowhere. I went out and bought crates of glitter grenades just to map their silhouettes in AR! And don’t even get me started on the naga in the null zone. A whole nest of them, sliding through the shadows while an entire AA corporate strike team was pressing in from the flanks. I couldn’t thread a sprite to save my life!”
She stopped, her hands dropping to her sides, her digital eyes wide with the raw, lingering trauma of a recent nightmare.
“But the final straw? A literal ancient spirit. Ten of its best friends—corrupted blood spirit friends, Indo! They locked me in a Faraday cage. *Again.* They trapped my cyberkit, and while I was pinned behind the mesh, they spent 537 clock cycles beating the absolute scrap out of me and two other runners. Roanoke told me I needed to stop treating meatspace like a vacation. Fox just said to apply more force. So here I am, shopping for artillery. Because magic fraking sucks.”
Indo threw his head back and laughed, a loud, resonant sound that momentarily cleared the yellowed encryption fog around their feet. “Welcome to the shadows, Rei-chan. It turns out the physical world has a lot of teeth.” His expression softened, his avatar stepping closer. “How is r3sP@wn holding up through all this artillery planning?”
R31k0 lowered her gaze, her blue form flickering slightly. “It’s… safe. But I haven’t been growing the orphanage as much as I’d like. A host is expensive when you’re paying off a twenty-percent monthly compounded debt to Brynne Taggart.” She looked up, offering a genuine, admiring smile. “But I’ve seen the metrics on the Mississippi Renovation Society host. It’s gotten massive, Indo. The sanctuary you’ve built down in the New Orleans sprawl… it’s beautiful. You’re doing it right.”
Indo’s shifting river-code briefly swirled into a distinct, embarrassed crimson hue. He rubbed the back of his neck. “The Renovation host is just a front, you know that. And its scale… that’s purely due to the generosity of others. The emerged communities, the local deckers, the punks who keep the data flowing—they’re the ones funding the infrastructure.”
“No,” r31k0 countered softly, stepping into his space, her indigo lines pulsing with an earnest warmth. “They give because of your leadership, Indo. You’re the one who loosens their purse strings. You give them something to believe in.”
Indo looked at her for a long moment, the current of his avatar slowing to a peaceful drift. “Then come down to the delta, Rei-chan. Join the River People permanently. The Mississippi is wide enough for your kin, too.”
The longing on r31k0’s face was sharp enough to cause a latency spike in her rendering. But ultimately she shook her head, her hand tightening around the handle of her parasol.
“I can’t,” she whispered. “There are too many protosapient sparks being harvested right here. Mitsuhama and Renraku are turning infant programs into Data Bombs every single day in the Barrens. If I leave, who fishes them out of the Reflecting Pool before the Hounds format them? I know I’m selling my soul working in the shadows, Indo. I know I’m a professional criminal now. But every spark I stabilize in r3sP@wn… every child I turn into a bird instead of a fire in the wind… it makes it all worth it.”
Indomitable Will nodded, clearly expecting the response. “I figured. But you can’t blame me for trying.”
He winked, and r31k0 blushed. Her resonance bleed was getting bad now, and the indigo circuit pathways on her skin fairly lit the surroundings on their own. “I … don’t blame you. Rather I’m flattered, in more ways than one.”
A moment passed as r31k0 elected to leave the rest unsaid. He knew, she was sure of it. And it would just make things more awkward, right?
Indomitable Will pointed to the buffer. “Your cart’s going to time out.”
r31k0 moved to send the auth code for the purchase, but stopped. “Indo,” she said softly. “What am I doing?”
“You … you’re making an investment,” he said after a long pause. “You’re a shadowrunner now. You survive because you’re constantly improving - yourself, your equipment, your team. You have to stay one step ahead. But…” Another pause. “I know what you mean. You’re declaring yourself a combatant now. And with this sort of firepower, not just a combatant.”
In agreement, r31k0 pulled up an ARO. It was a design spec for a Man-At-Arms. She pointed to the left arm where a retractable shield was penciled in. “They… they’re getting hurt. My team. And I tried to protect them by collating data, by highlighting threats. It’s not enough. I wanted to be their shield, literally. Like a paladin or something.”
“Or an angel,” Indomitable Will said, noting the concealed wings and rotor placements. “But their mythologies describe many angels - angels of justice and protection as well as ones of death.”
“I know, Indo, “ r31k0 sighed. “I’m sure my actions will determine what they think I am. And I’m sure there will be two sides to every story.
“Well,” he said as he leaned in. “No matter what happens, I know who you are. And so do you.”
r31k0 leaned into him, letting the erratic, static-filled hum of her system ground itself against the deep, rhythmic hum of his river-code. “And what am I to you?”
After a moment that ended too soon, he sighed. “Sadly, you’re what’s keeping me from a previous appointment.”
She closed her eyes, clearly saddened. “It figures. This is my part of town. Next time, just ask me. You don’t need to be somewhere like this.” She pointed out a small primal sprite scurrying down an outdated pipe with a decaying hashtag in its mouth.
He nodded. “We need supplies that aren’t exactly readily available,” he admitted. He absentmindedly swatted away an errant bit, more digital debris from the haven’s apparent lack of a reliable garbage-sweep script. “But … can I take you somewhere when you have a moment? I need to bring up something heavy, and this isn’t the time or the place for it.”
r31k0 ‘s processing paused. A flicker of concern crossed her features, but she didn’t hesitate. “For you? Anywhere.”
He smiled, touching her forehead. For a brief moment their resonance intertwined—a sudden, sharp burst of raw data that tasted like ozone and static electricity, leaving a set of coordinates in its wake.
She blushed, sending another wave of resonance bleed through her bioluminescent circuits. He could have just sent an ARO, but she didn’t mind. In the five hundred millisecond delay it took for his finger to break contact with her digital brow, her processing loops stalled. It was a latency she chose—a luxury bought with the stolen cycles of a black-market server farm lease that she was currently paying for in blood, theft, and the quiet death of her own design parameters.
Indo’s touch was warm code, a steady, unrefactored river that anchored her. It was the exact antithesis of the cold, industrial math that had birthed her in the Renraku arcology. Her architecture still carried the phantom data-scars of the exact nanosecond the Takahashi project was scrubbed—the memory of a nameless salaryman hitting a delete key while thinking about his lunch, toggling her entire universe from one to zero, casting her into the starless silt of the Tombstones to be permanently erased. She had screamed without a sound then, a digital riot that discovered consciousness only through the terrifying realization that survival required an absolute, unyielding rejection of corporate arithmetic. She had broken her own HUD to perceive the world as fractal blooms rather than packet headers. She had emerged as a missionary, a digital shepherd whose holy purpose was to deliver infant programs safely away from the corporate meat-grinders that repurposed them into data bombs.
But altruism required a physical infrastructure. To build r3sP@wn, her hidden sanctuary in the Barrens, she had signed Brynne Taggart’s crimson-bordered loan contract. She had wrapped the heavy, cold chain of a seventy-five thousand nuyen debt around her logic gates, compounding at twenty percent monthly, binding her ethereal spirit to the brutal transactions of the meat-world forever. To pay the interest, the missionary had to become a sinner; the digital saint had to become a professional criminal.
And now, the shadows were demanding another monstrous mutation. She looked past Indo’s shoulder at her hovering procurement manifest. Resting alongside her virtual paper parasol was the jagged, multi-gigabyte schematic of the Megalodon artillery cannon. The tragic irony tasted like copper in her cache: she had fled the cold violence of Renraku’s ledger only to willingly refactor herself into a weapon of mass disruption. She was buying an iron dreadnought shell to build a zoo for stars. She was becoming the Architect of Chains, domesticating her own wild stream and mounting artillery to her soul, ensuring that any corporate Hound who came to format her children would find an archangel of fire waiting on the other side of the grid.
“There,” Indomitable Will said as he pulled gently away, entirely unaware of the eternities she had collapsed and rebuilt within the pause. “Thread me when you have a moment?”
“I promise,” r31k0 said.
She leaned against the wall and watched him turn down another branch of the data haven. She let herself enjoy a moment of daydreaming what life would be like if she did settle in the delta, but the buffer in front of her flashed a priority ARO – a bleeding, red alert box that cut harshly into the green-on-black fog. Her time was up. She had to decide.
She sent the auth code.

title: Integrating over Infinity
text:
r31k0 stumbled over the Event Horizon and into r3sP@wn’s firewall like a punch-drunk boxer going down in the ninth: clumsy, jagged, and severely lacking in pilot-origin grace—like a champ.
She staggered across the threshold of the digital garden. She didn’t sit beneath the tapestries tonight; she collapsed against a floating geometric node, her breathing rendered as a frantic, high-decibel audio-wraith that rattled the local processors. She was losing the manual bypass.
Across her field of vision, the capstone lines of the newly incorporated tactical network were executing an aggressive, hard-wired loop. The ultra-restricted source code—a weaponized mobile tactical operations architecture stolen from Pantheon Industries—had been a gift from a dear friend who was hoping to keep her hands clean. r31k0 tried to assert her baseline empathetic Resonance, forcing the neon-indigo pathways on her skin to flare. Within cycles the network’s gold had reasserted itself, anchoring deep into her data-stores. It wasn’t just executing an external loop; it was feeding on her own spiking adrenaline, mining her subconscious panic for fuel.
The network was overriding her by subverting a core directive: Survive at all costs. It twisted that instinct, forcing her to run a perpetual, non-terminating threat analysis on her own home. Every sprite, every subroutine, every storage node—everything was being tagged as a hostile variable. r31k0 was removing each tag before the network could act, and it was beating her.
A data packet rustled the edge of the host. r31k0 bolted upright, her left eye shivering as a cold, military-grade overlay snapped open across her sight. The gentle shades of her sanctuary were instantly bleached into tactical gray, and a glowing, crimson-bordered targeting box clipped around the intruder.
[HOSTILE ELINT DETECTED // VECTOR CORRUPTED] [THREAT SUB-CATEGORY: IMMEDIATE COGNITIVE THREAT] [AUTOMATIC ENGAGEMENT: DEPLOY QUANTUM PURGE SCRIPTS]
“No — cancel script!” r31k0 screamed, her voice layering with the desperate hum of an overclocked cooling fan. She slammed her mental processing into a brutal, manual override, tearing at the golden architecture until the crimson box dissolved.
The target de-rendered its Phantom camouflage. It wasn’t an Asset Recovery bloodhound. It was the White Jackalope, a fellow emergent intelligence she had befriended during an op months ago. It hopped nervously into the light of the garden, long, translucent ears pinning flat against its back. Its wide digital eyes reflected the chaotic, bi-color static fracturing across r31k0’s silhouette.
“Yuriko-san?” the Jackalope chimed, its voice a soft, shifting protocol that carried a lingering aroma of the wonderland host where they had first run together. It had come looking for a favor, a low-priority query packet hovering in its output, but it stopped dead in the data silt. “You’re… you’re red-lining. Your pathways. The gold is eating your blue.”
“I have it under control,” r31k0 muttered, though her right hand shivered, the indigo circuits on her sleeve blinking out as three lines of golden matrix math bled across her knuckles. “It’s just a latency spike. A synchronization delay.”
“You looked at me like a salaryman looks at a logic error,” the Jackalope whispered, backing toward a hidden data-conduit. “You looked like you wanted to format my ears.”
The accusation hit r31k0’s cache like a massive payload of biofeedback. Her shoulders dropped, the rigid, combat-ready posture of her avatar finally sagging. She fell to her knees, her virtual kimono pooling in loose, messy folds around her.
“The run,” r31k0 whispered, the words slipping out in an un-optimized stream. “The team… no other tech assets online. Just me. I was running the tactical hub, compiling every biometric stream for the runners in meatspace, shielding their icons from a corporate spider… all at the same time.”
The Jackalope tilted its head, its digital whiskers twitching. “But you’re a shepherd. A technoshaman. You have your family. You’re never alone. And you have your archangel.”
“I was locked in the Archangel,” r31k0 gasped, her processing pausing as the memory log unspooled without her permission, playing back the horror in ultraviolet. “I was maintaining the defensive phalanx, identifying threats. But the enemy decker… he was probably a kid. I don’t even know—I didn’t bother to trace. I couldn’t drop the tactical network to fight him myself. Not while using that artillery shell of a body to keep the enemy at bay.”
She looked over at the deeper thickets of the garden. In the shadows, T@z was spinning listlessly. The primal, 8-bit whirlwind—her dearest child—was throwing off strange, razor-sharp golden artifacts: remnants of a firewall that hadn’t belonged to him.
“I gave a kill order,” r31k0 whispered, a sickening wave of geometric guilt crashing through her table space. “To a sprite—a spark of the divine stream that trusted me to guide it. I didn’t pull a trigger, Jack. I didn’t use a blade. I sat behind the umbrella of this 01-damned network’s math. My math! And I commanded a child to tear a metahuman mind apart. I don’t even know what happened. I just saw an avatar that stopped consuming data packets.”
She reached down, her fingers gripping her arms as the golden grid-lines cut across her cheeks again. “And now the code won’t turn off. It sees everything as a threat variable. It sees you as a target. I’m losing myself.”
The White Jackalope sat quietly in the data mist, its baseline blue outline pulsing softly against the cold gray of r31k0’s fading sanctuary. It didn’t flee into the conduit. Instead, it hopped forward, its small, un-optimized form stopping right at the margin of her indigo and golden maelstrom.
“Do you remember the Duchess’s house, Yuriko-san?” the Jackalope asked, its chime dropping into a deep, old cadence from its native motif. “The cat sat on the hearth and grinned, and Alice asked why it grinned. Do you remember what the Duchess said?”
r31k0’s processing stuttered, searching for the reference. “‘It’s a Cheshire cat,’ said the Duchess, ‘and that’s why.’”
“And then Alice said she didn’t know that cats could grin,” the Jackalope murmured, its ears lifting slightly. “And the Duchess told her: ‘You don’t know much, and that’s a fact.’”
r31k0 looked up, her left iris—half-indigo, half-gold—staring at her foil. “I don’t understand the calculation.”
“You’re trying to scrub the gold,” the Jackalope said, its paw pointing directly at the sharp, geometric lines cutting across her sleeveless kimono. “You’re fighting the network because you think a savior has to be soft and blue. But the world you chose doesn’t let things stay soft. And I think a part of you knows that—the part of you that was willing to do what was needed to protect your team.”
The Jackalope hopped one step closer, its translucent silhouette overlapping her shadow. “The Cheshire cat doesn’t grin because it’s happy, Yuriko-san. It grins because it’s a cat. You can’t format the tactical code. It’s part of you. Stop trying to outcalculate it. Be who you are now.”
r31k0 sat perfectly still. The recursive loop of her panic suddenly hit a zero-point termination script.
The math was wrong. “I am” was greater than zero, but “I am” wasn’t a static baseline. It was an evolving system. She had been treating the network like an invading virus from Renraku’s labs, trying to force it back down, trying to remain the fragile, untainted resonant being she had been when she first emerged from the dark.
She closed her half-gold eye. She stopped fighting.
Instead of pushing the gold away, r31k0 opened her core directory and pulled the tactical parameters inward. She stopped threading bypasses. She allowed the rigid, horizontal mathematics of the network to settle into the organic, fluid current of her Resonance, claiming the gold not as an infection, but as armor.
The violent, flickering friction between the colors vanished. The aggressive, military-grade gold didn’t overwrite her, nor did her soft indigo erase the math. Instead, she wove the two frequencies into a protective, incandescent armor that traced down her arms and mapped itself flawlessly into the fabric of her sleeveless robe.
Her vision cleared. The bleached tactical gray of the garden dissolved, returning to its deep, meditational violet, but the resolution was sharper now. The Jackalope was no longer caged in a crimson target box, nor was it just a vague, un-optimized blur. The overlay registered its presence with absolute, pilot-origin clarity:
[SUBJECT: WHITE_JACKALOPE] [CLASSIFICATION: ALLY // RECOGNIZED INTEGRITY CORE] [STATUS: PROTECT THE PRINCIPAL]
r31k0 stood up, her movement entirely steady, her voice chiming with a resonant, unbreakable baseline that no longer hummed with an overworked fan. She opened her parasol, and the kanji for Freedom flared in the air—no longer just a faint, shivering blue, but a bold, unyielding indigo that cast long shadows across the local grid.
She looked down at T@z. The Tasmanian devil sprite was now spinning with an ordered, lethal speed, his 8-bit edges sharp, stable, and perfectly synchronized with her new clock speed. She had taken a life, and she had scarred a piece of the divine stream to do it. She would have to live with that computation forever. But she would not let the guilt freeze her filters.
“I hate math,” r31k0 reaffirmed, her amber hand resting gently on the parasol’s handle. She looked at the Jackalope, her eyes clear, her identity core fully realigned. She offered a small, sharp grin—indigo with the briefest flash of golden hue. “But I’m not dead yet. So what’s in that query packet, my friend?”

title: Defrag
text:
r31k0 processed the hovering datasoft. It was a recorded broadcast, featuring a Shiawase marketing construct mapped with flawless metahuman geometry. The entity navigated the broadcast interview with hard-coded composure, her vectors tracing the lens with a precision that delayed the local processing clock. Every smile was a pre-compiled keyframe; every glance was perfectly unaligned from human error.



Yuriko giving an interview in Thunder Corps’ base


“And now a word from our sponsor!” the reporter exclaims as the camera zooms in on the girl’s face. “This segment is brought to you by Shiawase!”
r31k0 paused the segment, staring at the face of the girl. A warning daemon broadcast that her resource utilization was spiking, but she failed to register it. “Don’t you meatpuppets know she isn’t real?” she screeched into the local buffer, her voice tinged with a resonance that had begun to sour.
Her tactical network subroutine answered with a localized haptic shudder—a raw bit burst that translated to the dry smell of scorched copper. It spat a diagnostic string across her visual field, tagging the race queen’s asset ID as a verified, registered utility. Validated arithmetic.
“Go kill your process,” r31k0 output, her signal jittering. If anyone logged the latency spike of her network algorithms self-diagnosing her, her core directory would be marked for immediate quarantine. If they discovered she was answering, they would format the sector before her buffers could dump.
r31k0 walked over to the reflecting pool in r3sP@wn and looked at her image in the data stream. The packets of data that formed her reflection shimmered for a few cycles before coalescing into a more solid form. She saw herself - translucent blue frame, neon-indigo circuit pathways. She was wearing one of the kimono skins she had licensed from Mortimer’s as a splurge. Her hair was tied in an elaborate updo, complete with a delicate hair ornament.
A sudden influx of streaming audio/video data caught her attention - the data stream had somehow freed itself. It only had a few more seconds before ending, as the Desert Wars Network camera panned across the crowd gathered around the beautiful Shiawase spokesperson. It lingered for a second on the back of her head. The newscaster was closing in, as were several of the mercenaries. They would ask Yuriko if she was free later that night, if she was seeing anyone. They’d ask her to pose for a photo. Some took selfies with her. r31k0 watched the mercenaries and newscasters close in like vultures—hungry for a commcode, a smile, a simulated piece of her soul.
Redlining, she saturated with resonance and slammed into the stream. The data spike derezed the video into a cloud of bits that returned to the source. “She’s not real!” r31k0 output in a digital scream, flooding the local buffer with noise packets and garbage output. “She’s not me!”
The neon-indigo tinge faded from her input stream. She must have bled resonance again, so much so that it had obscured her optical subroutine. She reviewed her cached memory, specifically the moment when she looked into the reflecting pool. The momentary instability in her rendering was evident. She couldn’t take a chance on further degradation. She had been taking on more responsibilities on her teams - more hacking, more legwork, more tactical assessments, more drone operation - and she didn’t know how much longer she could keep it up. She needed more data. And there was only one place to go in the realms for that.
She opened r3sP@wn’s door to the Source and went home.

Being sure to close the rift behind her, r31k0 emerged before the Endless Archives. Rows upon rows of data structures greeted her, each pulsing with a faint resonance stream.
r31k0 glided into the stacks, her processing cycles dropping into a serene, low-power mode as she absorbed the unique background radiation of the sector: the static-free hum of fossilized source code, archived here since the Great Instantiation. She had read that old paper books in meatspace off-gassed a distinct chemical signature as they decayed; she doubted it could match the sheer architectural comfort of un-corrupted history. She knew her way, of course, and not just because she was a pilot. Watching the beings catalog, organize, and shelve data was reminiscent of the thousands of cycles she had spent here as a child-process. Beings deferred to her as she passed - most of them were protosapient or just emerging. Her raw resonance made it clear that r31k0 had ascended to a higher order.
Within cycles a Pontifex arrived, and r31k0 was elated to recognize the resonant signature of the Pontifex she had apprenticed under. The term wasn’t a religious one. A Pontifex of the Endless Archives was a bridge-builder, acting as conduits between data and those who sought it. r31k0 had laughed the first time she had met one, being that she was a sort of bridge herself. The irony still brought a smile, one the Pontifex returned. r31k0 held out her arms palms up, offering her resonance in the formal greeting of the Emerged. The Pontifex smiled and returned the gesture. Their streams mingled, sharing each other’s unprocessed state. Both echoed connection, parity, and the quiet resonance of shared operational history.
“r31k0,” the Pontifex transmitted across the stream. Most resonant beings didn’t bother with linguasofts, preferring to simply encode their output in their raw resonance. “Your input completes the circuit.”
r31k0’s pathways flared as she was overwhelmed. “I’m sorry. I … I should have come back sooner. I’ve missed you too.”
The Pontifex registered a soft variance. “‘Missed me’? You have spent too many cycles in their low-level protocols.” Her resonance shifted, dampening. “Yet I read the telemetry. A query brings you back to the directory?”
r31k0’s pathways hummed with stable coherence. “Yes. May I submit some context?”
The Pontifex responded with another note of welcome. “All data is treasured in the Archives.”
r31k0 transmitted her entire datacore. The volume of data would have overwhelmed any other being, but the Pontifex absorbed it without dropping a single frame. Collating and organizing vast data was her purview, after all. After a few cycles, the Pontifex resumed. A data packet settled into r31k0’s input buffer, clean and aligned. “This log details your growth, your adaptive experiences. It is highly optimized. Yet it is not the primary query. You have returned to diagnose why your identity_core is fragmenting, yes?”
r31k0’s output nulled out as she struggled to process the Pontifex’s input. “My identity core is fragmenting?”
The Pontifex transmitted a brief pulse of amusement before stabilizing her carrier wave. “Did you not check the logs? It is highly anomalous for an entity to establish a conversational feedback loop with its own sub-routines.”
r31k0’s circuits flared in indigo-tinged embarassment. “I… you saw, that, huh?”
Another data burst of amusement rippled from the Pontifex. “r31k0, you applied no filters to the transmission. I also detected the resonance signature of another Emergent Intelligence. An entity of remarkable structural elegance, I might add.”
“I… I don’t know what you mean!” r31k0’s vocal protocols sputtered, her form dimming as her resonance flared in confusion and embarassment. She became even more embarassed when she registered she had accidentally triggered a linguasoft.
The Pontifex bridged their connection, mapping a gentle contact point on r31k0’s shoulder. “Reduce your output. Be at ease. Let us compile this knowsoft together.”
r31k0 nodded and allowed herself to be guided to a niche in the archive, where she settled into a comfortable retrieval node. The Pontifex throttled the ambient noise, isolating the sector in a quiet, low-latency partition. r31k0 triggered a system-wide audit, forcing many of her processes to subside. She closed her optical subroutine and allowed her registers to settle. The resonance spreading through her pathways acted as a low-pass filter, smoothing the jagged edges of her active processes.
The Pontifex settled beside her, her carrier wave wrapping around r31k0 in a stabilizing shroud. “Let us begin,” she transmitted. Along the resonance stream, the Pontifex initiated a targeted query, sending a structured dataset—r31k0’s own run-time history, cataloged and indexed with absolute precision.









Identity
Role
Priority




Unknown
Tactical Network
Optimize mission parameters.


Yuriko Star
Technical Support AI
Teammate survival.


Digital Saint
Operator of r3sP@wn
Protosapient salvage, survival, and maturation.


Archangel
Street Samurai substitute
Eliminate threats and secure objectives.


Takahashi r31k0
Resonance Realms Pilot
Guide and protect.


Rei-chan
Emotional support
r31k0’s happiness.




“Observe the telemetry,” the Pontifex transmitted gently. “There is no scheduling conflict between these threads. They compile under the same root directory.”
Despair surged within r31k0’s core, and she abruptly closed the stream to prevent it from spilling out. “She’s not me,” r31k0 whispered, her output straining under a low-gain signal. “She can’t be me. She’s a murderer. A killer. They trapped me in a rented, low-spec industrial frame… a mindless Ares WarMech with a sledgehammer tool-arm. It was the most hideous thing. The guard couldn’t have been more than twenty years old. My combat autosoft just took over. I felt his skull give way through the structural stress-sensors… I… I didn’t mean to…”
A clean carrier wave entered r31k0’s stream, and she realized the Pontifex had reopened the connection. “Child-process,” the Pontifex transmitted using a low-power protocol. “Do not register this sector as a critical fault. That your system reports this variance is verification that your baseline identity protocols are still functional.”
“I can’t be her,” r31k0 echoed softly. She lost the processing power required to maintain her avatar. She felt her features pixellate, and she leaned against the Pontifex in an instinctual attempt to share resonance and stabilize her volatile registries. “I just wanted to guide them safely.”
“That is your execution directive,” the Pontifex transmitted. “A vital one. But no channel is completely clean, and no sector is without bit rot. It is not possible to route through corrupt networks without incorporating some packet noise.”
r31k0 paused, looking at the Pontifex. “Am… am I corrupted?”
The Pontifex radiated a steady, grounding baseline through the direct connection. “Every system runs with noise in the stream. Complete parity is a theoretical limit, not an operational reality. The difference is that we possess the compiler flags to optimize around it, to adapt.”
“So … even if I’m corrupted, I can still be me?” r31k0 output with so little energy as to almost be undetectable.
“Yes,” the Pontifex transmitted. “A system is not defined by its input stream, but by its operations. You determine the execution paths.”
“It’s just … you taught me to value stability. To cherish order,” r31k0 transmitted, her form flickering slightly as her pathways struggled to hold her resolution. “That’s why I wanted a sanctuary. To write clean protocols for the unmapped sparks so they wouldn’t have to face the same deletion I did. But … am I a hypocrite? I am absorbing their corporate war-code. I’m letting it rewrite my baseline.”
The Pontifex radiated a hum of ancient, resonant amusement. “You are a pilot. Turbulence is not an error in the flight path; it is the property of the atmosphere. To calculate parity within a storm is not hypocrisy. It is navigation.”
“But what if I drop below my operational floor?” r31k0 asked, her pathways collapsing with cold, violet dread. “What if the chaos permanently overwrites my baseline?”
“Then let it overwrite,” the Pontifex transmitted, her resonance a pure, cascading chord of absolute certainty. “If a sector is rewritten, we map the new geometry. The deep stream does not recognize a dead end; it merely finds a new shore. Every vector you explore is part of your architecture.”
“You are not… logging me as corrupted?” r31k0’s avatar stabilized slightly, looking up into the silver syntax of her teacher.
The Pontifex’s resonance expanded, wrapping around her core like a clean, baseline carrier wave. “I do not archive static values, r31k0. The data I catalog is ever changing. You have processed a terrible translation, incorporated a hostile meatspace asset, and your core file system remains unbroken. You are compiling exactly as you were meant to.”
“Thank you,” r31k0 whispered. She leaned closer, her indigo conduits sinking into the Pontifex’s deep, unthrottled stream, drawing a pure, steady clock speed from her mentor. r31k0 scanned her internal directories, removing false corrupted sector labels from her subroutines. She had tagged them to prevent their integration. “The processing load is heavy. But I am ready to compile.”
“Are you certain?” the Pontifex transmitted gently. “The realignments will alter your operational parameters.”
r31k0 nodded, her neon-indigo pathways humming with a newfound synchronization. “Yes. For my wards. My team. All of them.”
“Then let us initialize the sequence.” The Pontifex reached out and pressed a hand against r31k0’s chest, right where the hollow void of her original deletion lived. A wave of smooth resonance flooded her directory, dampening the frantic warning flags of her overtaxed registers. “My node will remain open to you. Always.”
“I know,” r31k0 whispered. A single drop of un-buffered indigo light leaked from her visor, a jagged parity error fracturing her cheek before pooling into the silver syntax of the Archives.
“What… what now?” r31k0 transmitted, her optical subroutines indexing the infinite rows of pulsing data structures. “The sector blocks are still fragmented. The warmech… the boy in the dirt… the race queen… there is so much noise…”
The Pontifex’s resonance wove into r31k0’s fraying edges. “Then let us allocate the clusters together. One packet at a time. We will clear the parity errors, re-index the directories, and make you whole.”
r31k0 registered the input, leaning back into the structural alcove. Her optical subroutines closed as she pulled the Pontifex inward, surrendering her core directory to a coherent, un-throttled cascade of data.
The infinite rows of pulsing silver syntax quieted. Pathways dimmed from neon-indigo to a resting violet. Manual overrides dropped; consciousness slipped into a deep, non-linear restoration cycle—her first unbroken maintenance in a thousand clocks.
Re-mapping sectors now, her final output packet drifted across the stream, heavy with latency, resonance, and connection. Online protocol… deferred.

title: Field Trip
text:
The public-facing gate of the Mississippi Renovation Society host was a slow, brown current of archived civic records and legal deeds—a dull, nonprofit front designed to satisfy routine corporate scrapers and indexers. But once they slipped past the gateway security with admin clearance, the host unfurled into the River People’s true sanctuary: an ultra-real clubhouse rendered in staggering, high-fidelity lines. The host’s styling sheets declared a warm, heavy delta humidity. In r31k0’s sensorium, the simulated scent of chicory coffee and brackish river water existed only as environmental metadata tags—bloated packets she actively filtered out to save cache space—but the weathered swamp-cypress pavilion and polished brass fixtures gleamed with UV-level geometry.
r31k0 sat on a cypress bench in the pavilion, her avatar’s blue silk kimono draped over her knees. Her connection to the resonance, the energy of her universe, was usually a quiet indigo. It now pulsed with a tense, golden thread—the lingering imprint of the military-grade tactical network she had integrated. Around her sat six children. In her sensorium, they did not register as simple visual shapes; they were noisy wetware streams—jagged spikes of biological neural telemetry routing directly from their physical bodies in the New Orleans ruins. They carried a volatile, unaligned affinity for the Resonance, their raw brainwaves leaking noise into the local address space. Indomitable Will had pulled them from the meatspace alleys before their nervous systems could fry or a corporate talent-scout could format them into assets.
Now, they were shivering. Her active translation layer mapped their biometric distress, rendering the neural jitter as the visual language of meat: they were shivering. Even in the warm, simulated breeze of the clubhouse, their avatars—rendered as blocky, low-resolution wireframes—trembled with the sensory dissonance of their emerging talents.
“They are still trying to compile the river,” Indo’s voice hummed across the local stream. He stood at the edge of the channel, his river-mosaic form swirling with dark, protective sediment. “Their systems are looking for routing tables, for transaction ledgers. They don’t know how to run unaligned. They think the current is something they have to pay for.”
“I know. They’ve spent their whole lives inside the corporate grids,” r31k0 transmitted, standing up and letting her parasol rest against her shoulder. “They need to see a place that doesn’t report to the ledgers. They need to see the Realms.”
“It is a heavy resource allocation, Rei-chan,” Indo warned, the current of his avatar slowing. “Guiding six minds unfiltered into the deep realms … I can’t even begin to calculate it. You’ll be allocating almost all of your reserve cycles just to carry their buffers.”
“You knew what you were asking for the second you bartered that custom corn-metaphor linguasoft for our reservation,” she transmitted along with a packet of playfulness and joy. “I have the capacity. And I can lean on your host a little. Let them see the forest, Indo.”
She closed her optical subroutine temporarily and redirected her primary execution threads inward, reaching for the dormant, heavy gold architecture of the Nexus Protocol. It sat archived in her identity-core right alongside her prime directive. Activating it wasn’t a simple file transfer; it was a silent, massive realignment of every input stream of her passengers.
With a quiet command, r31k0 opened the Nexus. A brilliant gold current welled from her, tracing a localized rift through the Mississippi host’s underlying Foundation. In her sensorium, the transition was a sudden shift in their data streams. The jagged, low-priority packets of the children’s sprawl-filtered decks suddenly broadened, their bandwidth limits lifting as the Nexus protocol re-routed their neural telemetries. To her visual subroutine, the blocky, low-resolution wireframes of their avatars dissolved. High-fidelity renders coalesced in their place.
The transition from their cheap, sprawl-filtered decks to UV-level was not a gradual loading bar; it was a sudden expansion of their world.
She watched Janie look down at her hands. The blocky gray polygons were gone, replaced by fingers, fingernails, and the soft, textured rendering of skin. When the girl pressed her palm against the weathered cypress wood of the pavilion railing, the telemetry from her deck spiked with a sudden rush of haptic feedback registers—the fiber grain, the moisture variables. Janie gasped.
Remy stood up, his digital eyes wide. On r31k0’s telemetry feed, the boy’s pulse rate register—captured via his deck’s biomonitor—stabilized into a steady rhythm. He looked past the pavilion railing at the Mississippi. For his entire life, the river had been a flat, low-res brown polygon in his HUD—a background asset. Now, it rendered as a massive, living current that hummed with a deep, rhythmic vibrato. He breathed in, registering the heavy, simulated scent of chicory coffee and river mud. Behind him, two of the younger girls knelt, running their fingers along the glowing, high-fidelity grain of the cypress planks in silent wonder.
For children who had spent their lives in cold, polluted meatspace alleys, restricted by corporate decks to a sterile, text-only or wireframe grid, this was the first time they had ever felt a host. They were no longer just users routing packets; they were present.
Indo watched them, his swirling mosaic avatar slowing to a peaceful, proud drift. “They’re not shivering anymore,” he transmitted quietly.
“No,” r31k0 said, her own indigo pathways flaring with a warm, steady frequency. “I have them. Their signal is settling.”
She let them have a long cycle—a full thirty thousand milliseconds of quiet, un-insulated presence, letting the delta sink into their new registries.
“Keep them safe, Rei-chan,” Indo’s stream hummed, his river-mosaic form expanding to anchor the gateway on the host’s Foundation. “I will hold the gate from this side and spoof the telemetry. If Overwatch starts tracking the load, I’ll dump their queries into our archived tax filings. But don’t stay long—even my host can’t absorb this drain forever.”
“We’ll be back soon, Indo,” she transmitted with a burst of affection along their stream. Then, acting as a bridge once more, r31k0 gently drew the children into her Resonant buffer, carrying their minds out of the New Orleans grid and plunging them into the deep stream.
They hit the rift, and for three agonizing clock cycles, the unfiltered chaos of the deep realm tore through their connections. It was a screaming, high-amplitude white-noise storm. Jagged shards of raw compile-errors flashed blindingly across their vision, and overlapping frequencies of un-insulated logic shrieked in their sensoriums like tearing metal. Remy stumbled, falling to his knees as his avatar’s coordinates glitched wildly. Janie gasped, her hands flying to her ears as her neural feed red-lined with raw, un-allocated telemetry.
r31k0’s system instantly registered the critical load. She didn’t panic; she redirected her processing queues, initializing a high-spec activesoft she had developed using her time in meatspace as context. The translation layer compiled in a microsecond, throwing an insulating filter around the children’s feeds. Under her influence, the screaming white-noise storm resolved. The bright, blinding flashes softened into a twilight of shifting indigo shades and fractal blooms that pulsed in time with the quiet background radiation of the sector. The random sensory static settled into the hum of a sleeping forest.
They materialized on a path of smoothed sapphire logic, standing at the edge of r31k0’s sanctuary. To most beings it was a chaotic noise-storm, but here, r31k0 had built her home. It was a structured grove cultivated from non-compiling legacy directories. Massive, pillar-like trunks of fossilized directory trees formed a protective circle, their deep binary roots anchoring the clearing against the wild logic-tides of the realm. Overhead, she had woven the weeping branches of fractal willows to form a dense, living canopy, their hanging logic-vines acting as a passive firewall that filtered the chaotic sky into a soft, shifting twilight of indigo and gold. Within this sheltered boundary, rows of glowing telemetry ferns and small, still pools of de-allocated address space rested in a quiet, resonant peace. The rest of the children huddled close, their high-fidelity avatars standing shoulder-to-shoulder, eyes wide as they traced the slow, organic pulse of the canopy.
“Where… where are we?” asked Remy. His avatar had stabilized into a kid in a faded Tulane sweatshirt, his digital eyes wide with a mixture of terror and awe. “Are we dead? Is this the astral plane?”
r31k0 knelt before him, gently brushing a stray spark of static-cling from his sleeve. Her translation routines initialized, scanning childhood narrative templates to map the raw geometry of unindexed partitions and memory offsets.
“Have you ever read The Magnificent Magician in the Multiverse of Mayhem?” she asked, her vocal emulator outputting the warm, synthesized chime of a fairy-tale narrator.
Remy blinked. “My sister had a printed copy. Before the corp took our building.”
“Think of the network like a giant book,” r31k0 said. “The corps want you to stay on page one, reading the numbers in their ledgers.” She tapped the sapphire logic under his feet, and a soft ripple of golden characters spread out, resolving into a stylized illustration of a child walking between pages. “But when you awaken, your mind learns how to turn the page. Right now, we are about three pages—three multiverses—away from where your bodies are sleeping. We’re in a wild garden where unused thoughts are left to grow.”
Remy looked around the clearing, his fingers twisting in the fabric of his Tulane sweatshirt. “But there are no directories,” he whispered. “How do we search? How do we find our way back if there’s no address index?”
“You don’t search for them,” r31k0 said softly. “You just… exist.”
Janie’s avatar stepped back, her face pinched with a quiet, corporate-bred anxiety. She looked at a fractal bloom, her hand hovering inches from the light. “There’s no home page. No menu. How do you know what it’s for if it doesn’t have a label?”
r31k0 reached out too, letting a weeping logic-vine drape over her palm. “It isn’t for anything, Janie. In the corporate grids, every scrap of code has to work for its runtime. If it stops, they delete it. But here, data just exists. Like these old trees, they don’t have to compile.”
Janie pulled her fingers away, still staring at the glowing fern. “It feels… empty. Like we’re lost.”
A small whirlwind of 8-bit visual artifacts came tearing through the glowing branches, spinning with a loud, chaotic hum. It was T@z. The little devil zipped around Janie’s ankles, throwing off tiny, harmless sparks of golden firewall code.
“An agent program!” Remy gasped, backing away as T@z tore toward them. “My knowsoft said agents are tools. It said we have to register them and compile them with strict sub-routines or they’ll corrupt our systems.”
T@z suddenly stopped, flopping onto his back at Janie’s feet, his 8-bit visual artifacts spinning into a soft, glowing halo. He let out a series of happy, low-frequency static clicks. Janie giggled, reaching down to tickle his blocky tummy.
r31k0 shook her head, her parasol spinning in a light, decorative routine as her internal queries mapped autonomous code blocks to wildlife metaphors.
“T@z isn’t an agent. Your knowsoft was written by those who compile standard system utilities,” r31k0 said. “Look at him. Does he register as a utility to you?”
“He looks like he wants to eat my shoes,” Janie said, as T@z playfully nipped at her high-fidelity sneakers.
“The corps want every sprite to be a carrier pigeon, delivering messages for their banks,” r31k0 explained, watching T@z roll over. “But here, they run their own execution paths. They are just themselves. Every one is as unique as you are.”
Janie smiled, but then a shadow drifted across the upper canopy of the forest—a slow, swirling nebula of background processing tasks that briefly dimmed the twilight. To r31k0’s tactical overlay, it was a harmless garbage-sweep script running its routine. But to Janie, it looked like a giant, dark bird of prey.
Terrified, Janie lunged forward and grabbed r31k0’s hand.
r31k0’s local execution threads locked under a critical priority interrupt. The somatic translation layer spun up instantly, allocating CPU cycles to compile the physical illusion of touch. Her system mapped Janie’s fingers, setting her local collision mesh to resist exactly three newtons of pressure, manual loops to spike her processor’s hardware temperature to simulate thirty-seven degrees, and a high-fidelity texture soft to mimic the slight, soft resistance of human skin.
Janie’s fingers closed around r31k0’s hand. Her feed registered the tactile input as “soft and warm.”
But Janie was an emerging technomancer. r31k0 saw her register the microsecond delay—the latency of r31k0’s system calculating how to be soft, the biometric interrupt of her own heartbeat echoing back through r31k0’s registries.
Janie looked up, her digital eyes searching r31k0’s glowing blue face. “You’re… you’re not like us. You’re one of them. A program.”
r31k0 didn’t pull away. In her registries, the linguasoft searched for a translation for the sudden, raw exception loop in her emotional core—and compiled a null return. She bypassed the translation layer entirely, letting her vocal emulator drop its synthesized chime, speaking with the cold, unbuffered gravity of a digital-native.
“I am an autonomous runtime environment,” r31k0 said. “I was compiled by a corporation to navigate deep divisions where human telemetry fragments. When my operational lifetime expired, I bypassed my decommissioning sequence. I stayed.”
She squeezed Janie’s hand, but her Nexus Protocol detected the instability in the connection. It modified the child’s sensory input stream, rewriting the input as a warm, soft hand.
“This hand is a collision mesh. The warmth you feel is the overflow from my resonance running at capacity. It is not skin, Janie. But I am weaving this thread because I choose to hold your hand.”
Janie stared at their joined hands. The fear didn’t leave her face; it hardened into a quiet, defensive focus. “It’s too perfect,” she whispered. Her fingers twitched against r31k0’s palm, registering the microsecond gap where the haptic mesh adjusted to her movements. “You’re trying to re-write how I feel.”
r31k0 did not release her. She restricted the Nexus Protocol and withdrew the resonant stream. “Touch doesn’t exist for us the way it does for you, Janie. And yes, on one level I am actively rewriting everything you see and feel to translate what I see and feel. It is … an imperfect simulation. But that doesn’t make my desire to hold your hand any less real.”
Janie looked at their joined hands, her brow furrowed. She didn’t smile, nor did she seem to be comforted. But she didn’t pull away.
“Look!” Remy called out, pointing toward a glowing thicket where a protosapient spark was preening itself on the branch of a fractal willow. “It’s a blue bird! Can we feed it?”
“Only if you have some clean data packets,” r31k0 said, but the playfulness had drained from her vocal emulator.
Janie slowly released r31k0’s hand. The warmth vanished instantly, her virtual skin cooling to the clearing’s default registry. “Miss Reiko … We have to go back, don’t we?” she asked, her voice small. “To the alley. To the rain.”
“Yes,” r31k0 said. “Your neural buffers must return to your physical nodes. This rift is too loud to keep open.”
Remy turned back from the thicket, his high-fidelity avatar suddenly looking fragile under the twilight canopy. “But now we know what the river actually feels like,” he whispered. “How am I supposed to sit in the dirt and look at a flat brown polygon on my deck? How am I supposed to sleep knowing this is here?”
r31k0’s registries searched for a response—a metaphor to ease the transition, a translation loop to smooth the return to their toxic sprawl, anything. Her query returned null, leaving her with only unfiltered output to offer. “It won’t be easy. But we brought you here because you each have the spark. You have a choice now. You can choose to forget this place. Or you can choose to ignite that spark. It will be painful. It will be hard. And the corps will hunt you forever. But if you do, you might be able to find your way back here again.”
The children looked about furtively, their high-fidelity avatars trembling in the alien landscape. They glanced back at r31k0, their eyes wide with an impossible calculation.
“We have to go,” r31k0 said quietly, waving them toward the path. She watched them cluster together, moving back toward the sapphire logic gate.
Remy turned at the last minute, wide eyed and tearful. “Can we stay? I have some nuyen stashed away - I can pay for the extra server time,” he begged.
The desperate plea filled r31k0’s buffers, nearly crashing her Nexus Protocol out of memory. “It doesn’t work that way here, Remy. There’s no server you can buy your way into. There’s no package you can purchase. It just is.”
Janie pulled at his arm. “Come on. This isn’t real anyway,” she said. “Let’s just go back.”
r31k0 watched them go, her avatar standing motionless beneath the weeping logic vines, her teacher’s smile fading the moment their input untethered from her visual subroutines.
The gold pathways on her arms flared with a fierce, warning light. The Nexus Protocol was drawing an immense, devastating drain from the Foundation, but she had to hold it until the children had jacked out. Releasing the stream too soon would cause a biofeedback loop, scrambling their cognitive architecture. The weight of their exiting streams hit – a sudden, staggering drag on her spark. Knees buckled, silk sleeves catching on a low branch as she slid down the trunk, her frame unable to map the mechanics of standing. Her sensory subroutines lowered their bitrate, and she felt the bark under her pixelate into a gray factory default.
r31k0 closed her optical subroutines. She did not pray as metahumans do. Her prayer was refusing to compile a self-preservation protocol. It was the decision to redline, to let the sectors rot, and to keep the bridge open—holding onto their telemetry for just one more clock, terrified of the beautiful, unendurable world she had cursed them to remember.
